Acne.description: Acne vulgaris - chronic inflammatory pilosebaceous disorder

immediate_care: Commence twice-daily facial cleansing with non-comedogenic cleanser (pH 4.5-5.5)
Initiate topical benzoyl peroxide 2.5% or salicylic acid 2% once daily
Apply non-occlusive, oil-free moisturizer post-cleansing
medications: Benzoyl peroxide 2.5% once daily (bacteriostatic, keratolytic)
Alternatively: Salicylic acid 2% lotion once daily (β-hydroxy acid)
If irritation develops, reduce frequency to alternate days
environmental_precautions: Avoid occlusive cosmetics and heavy moisturizers
Use non-comedogenic sunscreen SPF 30+ daily (essential with topicals)
Minimize contact with irritants; avoid excessive face touching
lifestyle_adjustments: Regular sleep schedule (7-9 hours) supports skin barrier function
Reduce dairy and high-glycemic foods if triggers identified
Manage stress through proven relaxation techniques
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If lesions persist beyond 8-12 weeks of consistent topical therapy
If inflammation worsens or signs of secondary infection appear
For evaluation if nodular or cystic components develop

immediate_care: Initiate combination therapy: benzoyl peroxide + topical retinoid
Cleanse twice daily with gentle, non-irritating cleanser
Schedule dermatology consultation within 2-3 weeks
medications: Benzoyl peroxide 5-10% once daily (bacteriostatic agent)
Topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.025%, adapalene 0.1%, or tazarotene 0.05%) at bedtime
Start retinoid 2-3 times weekly, titrate frequency as tolerance permits
Consider oral antibiotics (doxycycline 100mg daily) if widespread comedones
Benzoyl peroxide + retinoid combination minimizes antibiotic resistance
environmental_precautions: Avoid direct sunlight 4 hours post-retinoid application
Use broad-spectrum SPF 50+ daily (retinoids increase photosensitivity)
Eliminate all occlusive products; verify comedogenicity ratings
lifestyle_adjustments: Dietary modification: reduce simple carbohydrates, dairy, and omega-6 fats
Sleep optimization: 7-8 hours nightly enhances skin barrier recovery
Stress management crucial; consider cognitive behavioral approaches
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If no improvement after 6-8 weeks of combination therapy
For evaluation of oral antibiotic responsiveness at 3 months
If signs of severe nodular acne or post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation
warnings: Retinoid: teratogenic; ensure contraception if reproductive age
Retinoid: expect initial 2-4 week purging phase with increased breakouts

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology referral required within 48-72 hours
Do not delay: assess for candidacy for isotretinoin (Accutane)
Initiate aggressive anti-inflammatory measures immediately
medications: Consider isotretinoin (0.5-1 mg/kg/day) for nodular/cystic acne with scarring risk
Alternative: Oral antibiotics (doxycycline or minocycline 100mg daily) + topical retinoid + benzoyl peroxide
Add oral contraceptives if female (improves hormonal acne)
Spironolactone 50-100mg daily if androgenic component suspected
Topical benzoyl peroxide 10% + tretinoin 0.05% at bedtime
environmental_precautions: Strict photoprotection: SPF 50+ mineral-based sunscreen, reapply q2h
Avoid all occlusive cosmetics and fragranced products
Consider physical sun protection (wide-brimmed hat, lightweight clothing)
lifestyle_adjustments: Low-glycemic index diet; eliminate dairy completely for 8-12 weeks trial
Sleep: 8+ hours nightly; optimize sleep quality
Stress reduction: consider psychotherapy given psychological impact
when_to_seek_medical_attention: URGENT if signs of systemic inflammation (fever, lymphadenopathy)
If isotretinoin initiated: monthly dermatology follow-up mandatory
For evaluation of depressive symptoms (isotretinoin-associated mood changes)
warnings: Isotretinoin: absolute contraindication in pregnancy; iPLEDGE program mandatory
Isotretinoin: monthly lab monitoring (LFTs, lipids, pregnancy test)
Risk of severe scarring if untreated; early aggressive intervention critical

Melanoma.description: Basal Cell Carcinoma - most common skin cancer, low metastatic potential but locally invasive

immediate_care: URGENT: Excisional biopsy with 4-6mm margins within 1-2 weeks
Histological confirmation mandatory before definitive treatment
Do not delay; assess Mohs micrographic surgery candidacy (especially face/critical areas)
medications: Surgical excision with 4mm margins (non-Mohs standard); 95% cure rate
Mohs micrographic surgery if: face, ear, nose, periocular, hands, feet (optimal cosmetic outcome)
Histopathological type assessment: nodular, superficial, or infiltrative (infiltrative requires wider margins)
environmental_precautions: Strict photoprotection: SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen daily, reapply q2 hours
Avoid sun 10 AM - 4 PM; wear protective clothing (wide-brimmed hat, long sleeves)
Monthly self-skin examination; photograph lesion for surveillance
lifestyle_adjustments: Smoking cessation if applicable
Vitamin D supplementation (cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU daily)
Stress management: cancer diagnosis carries psychological burden
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Post-operative: if signs of infection (purulence, erythema, warmth)
Annual dermatology surveillance indefinitely; increased risk of second BCC
warnings: CRITICAL: Excision/Mohs surgery mandatory; no topical treatments effective
Cure rates 90-95% with appropriate surgical management; delay increases morbidity

immediate_care: STAT: Excisional biopsy or Mohs micrographic surgery within 48-72 hours
Histological subtype determination critical: infiltrative pattern requires aggressive treatment
Assess for perineural invasion or deep invasion (risk factors for recurrence)
medications: Mohs micrographic surgery strongly recommended (95-99% cure vs 90-95% standard excision)
Standard excision: 6mm margins if nodular; 8-10mm if infiltrative pattern
Post-operative: imiquimod 5% cream if surgical margins inadequate (5x weekly x 6 weeks alternative)
environmental_precautions: Complete photoprotection: SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen q2h, protective clothing mandatory
Avoid sun entirely if possible 10 AM - 4 PM; utilize physical barriers
Monthly self-examination with photographic documentation
lifestyle_adjustments: Smoking cessation critical; smoking impairs wound healing and immune surveillance
Optimal nutrition to support wound healing post-surgery
Psychological support for cancer-related anxiety
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Post-operative wound surveillance: weekly x 4 weeks
Annual dermatology surveillance indefinitely; cumulative sun exposure increases recurrence risk
If any recurrence signs detected (growth at surgical site)
warnings: Infiltrative and perineural invasion patterns carry significantly higher recurrence risk
Mohs micrographic surgery preferred for locations with functional/aesthetic importance

immediate_care: EMERGENCY: Surgical consultation within 24-48 hours; assess for invasion into deeper structures
Consider staging imaging if invasion into underlying tissues/bone suspected
Wide local excision (>10mm margins) or Mohs micrographic surgery urgent
medications: Mohs micrographic surgery STRONGLY RECOMMENDED (highest cure rate 99%)
If bone/cartilage involvement: consider Mohs followed by reconstruction with skin graft/flap
Adjuvant radiation therapy if margins inadequate or extensive perineural invasion
environmental_precautions: Obsessive photoprotection: SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen q60-90 minutes
Complete avoidance of sun 10 AM - 4 PM; utilize sun-protective garments
Monthly self-examination; consider photography for surveillance
lifestyle_adjustments: Smoking cessation mandatory; impairs wound healing and immune function
Nutritional optimization: high-protein diet for post-operative healing
Mental health support essential; extensive surgery may impact body image
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Post-operative surveillance: weekly x 8 weeks, then monthly x 3 months
Quarterly dermatology surveillance for first 2 years, then annual indefinitely
IMMEDIATELY if signs of recurrence or new lesions develop
warnings: Deep invasion, bone/cartilage involvement increases morbidity significantly
Adjuvant radiation may be necessary; discuss with radiation oncologist
High recurrence risk; intensive long-term surveillance non-negotiable

Eczema.description: Atopic dermatitis - chronic inflammatory condition with impaired skin barrier

immediate_care: Optimize skin barrier function: apply ceramide-based moisturizer within 3 minutes of bathing
Identify and eliminate specific allergen/irritant triggers through systematic elimination
Use fragrance-free, dye-free emollients (CeraVe, Eucerin, Aveeno Eczema Therapy)
medications: Topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1% or triamcinolone 0.1%) once daily for acute flares
Maintain skin barrier: ceramide-enriched moisturizer twice daily
Consider tacrolimus 0.03% ointment as steroid-sparing alternative for sensitive areas
environmental_precautions: Avoid identified triggers: harsh soaps, fragrances, sulfates, lanolin, wool
Use only fragrance-free, pH-neutral cleansers
Maintain environmental humidity 30-50%; consider humidifier in dry climates
lifestyle_adjustments: Bathing: lukewarm water (not hot) for 5-10 minutes maximum
Pat dry gently (not rubbing); apply moisturizer immediately to damp skin
Dietary assessment: consider elimination diet if food triggers suspected (dairy, eggs, nuts, wheat)
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If flares worsen despite barrier optimization within 2 weeks
For signs of secondary bacterial infection (purulence, fever, lymphadenopathy)
If significant sleep disruption or quality-of-life impact
warnings: Allergen avoidance essential; relapse likely with trigger re-exposure
Long-term topical corticosteroid use risks skin atrophy; limit to affected areas, shortest duration

immediate_care: Aggressive barrier repair: ceramide/colloidal oatmeal-based moisturizer application q4-6h
Identify specific allergen triggers through history and patch testing if unclear
Initiate topical corticosteroid (mometasone 0.1% or fluticasone 0.005%) twice daily for 1-2 weeks
medications: Topical corticosteroid: mometasone furoate 0.1% ointment twice daily x 1-2 weeks, then taper
Topical calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment or pimecrolimus 1% cream) as maintenance
Consider antihistamine (cetirizine 10mg daily) if pruritus severely impacts sleep
Oral antibiotics if signs of secondary infection (cephalexin 500mg QID x 7 days)
environmental_precautions: Strict allergen avoidance: eliminate identified triggers completely
Humidification: maintain 40-60% relative humidity; use humidifier in dry environments
Avoid occlusive clothing; wear cotton or silk undergarments
Laundry: use hypoallergenic detergent (free & clear, ALL free), extra rinse cycle
lifestyle_adjustments: Sleep optimization critical; severe pruritus may require antihistamine for sleep maintenance
Stress management: stress-induced exacerbations common; consider cognitive behavioral therapy
Dietary modification: eliminate suspected triggers (milk, eggs, peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish if atopic history)
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If inadequate response to topical therapy within 2-3 weeks; consider systemic steroids
For signs of widespread involvement or severe pruritus affecting function
If secondary infection suspected or present (requires antibiotic coverage)
warnings: Potent topical corticosteroid tachyphylaxis: rotate agents, use lowest potency
Severe cases may require systemic immunosuppression; early escalation prevents chronicity

immediate_care: Consider systemic immunosuppression: dupilumab (IL-4Rα antagonist) or cyclosporine
Acute flare management: short course oral corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg daily x 7-14 days) if widespread
Barrier repair: intensive emollient therapy (petroleum jelly, ointment-based products) hourly
medications: Biologic therapy STRONGLY RECOMMENDED: dupilumab 600mg SC loading, then 300mg q2 weeks
Alternative: cyclosporine 3-5 mg/kg/day divided BID (requires renal function/BP monitoring)
Topical: potent corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05%) BID for 1-2 weeks, then taper to lower potency
Topical calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus 0.1%) twice daily as maintenance/steroid-sparing agent
Antihistamine: hydroxyzine 25-50mg TID for severe pruritus; consider low-dose doxepin (10-25mg) at bedtime
environmental_precautions: Complete environmental trigger elimination; detailed allergen assessment via patch testing
Humidification: maintain 50-70% relative humidity; ultrasonic humidifier recommended
Climate: consider temporary relocation to humid environment if severe (coastal regions)
Bathing: only gentle, fragrance-free emollients; avoid all soaps and cleansers
lifestyle_adjustments: Sleep: critical; may require sedating antihistamine or low-dose antidepressant for sleep maintenance
Work: may require occupational modifications if environmental triggers present at workplace
Psychological support: depression/anxiety common with severe eczema; address proactively
Dietary elimination: comprehensive food allergy testing; elimination diet trial if indicated
when_to_seek_medical_attention: MANDATORY: Dermatology consultation within 1 week; assess biologic therapy candidacy
Monthly follow-up if systemic therapy initiated
EMERGENCY if signs of erythroderma (>80% body surface involvement) or sepsis
warnings: Systemic corticosteroids should be SHORT-TERM ONLY; chronic use contraindicated
Dupilumab: monitor for conjunctivitis (20-40% incidence); manage with antihistamines/topical steroids
Cyclosporine: requires regular monitoring (BP, renal function, LFTs); baseline ophthalmology exam

Psoriasis.description: Psoriasis vulgaris - chronic immune-mediated inflammatory dermatosis

immediate_care: Gentle emollient therapy: apply thick moisturizer (Eucerin, CeraVe) immediately post-bathing
Initiate topical corticosteroid (triamcinolone 0.1% or fluticasone 0.005%) once daily
Address triggering factors: stress reduction, infection prevention, medication review
medications: Topical corticosteroid: fluticasone propionate 0.005% cream once daily (low potency, minimal systemic absorption)
Keratolytic agent: salicylic acid 2% or urea 20% to facilitate scale removal
Topical vitamin D analogue (calcitriol 3 mcg/g) as adjunctive or steroid-sparing agent
Emollient: ceramide-rich moisturizer BID-TID (CeraVe, Aveeno, Eucerin Eczema)
environmental_precautions: Stress reduction: meditation, yoga, or psychotherapy (stress-induced exacerbations common)
Sun exposure: controlled UVB exposure beneficial; limit to 15-20 minutes daily initially
Avoid triggers: beta-blockers, NSAIDs, abrupt corticosteroid withdrawal
Humidity: maintain 40-60% relative humidity; humidifier in dry climates
lifestyle_adjustments: Sleep: 7-9 hours nightly; poor sleep exacerbates psoriasis
Infection prevention: prompt treatment of streptococcal infections (guttate psoriasis risk)
Alcohol avoidance: ethanol consumption associated with flare exacerbation
Weight management: obesity associated with increased severity; consider gradual weight reduction
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If inadequate response to topical therapy within 4-6 weeks
For evaluation of nail involvement or arthropathy signs
If widespread involvement develops (>10% BSA)
warnings: Abrupt corticosteroid withdrawal may precipitate rebound flare or pustular psoriasis
Taper topical corticosteroids gradually; use lowest potency for prolonged therapy

immediate_care: Initiate combination topical therapy: potent corticosteroid + vitamin D analogue
Consider phototherapy (narrowband UVB) if extensive trunk/extremity involvement
Address systemic triggering factors: infections, stress, medications
medications: Topical corticosteroid: mometasone furoate 0.1% or clobetasol 0.05% twice daily x 2 weeks, then taper
Vitamin D analogue: calcipotriene 0.005% ointment once daily as maintenance/steroid-sparing
Keratolytic: salicylic acid 3% or urea 40% cream for scale removal
Phototherapy: narrowband UVB (311 nm) 3x weekly if extensive involvement (>10-15% BSA)
environmental_precautions: Comprehensive stress reduction program: CBT, mindfulness, exercise
Infection prevention: monitor for streptococcal throat infections; prompt antibiotic treatment
Medication review: discontinue beta-blockers if feasible; avoid NSAIDs
Climate: sun exposure beneficial; 15-30 minutes daily UVB exposure recommended
lifestyle_adjustments: Sleep optimization: melatonin or short-acting sleep aid if insomnia present
Alcohol cessation: complete avoidance if psoriasis-alcohol association identified
Weight loss: gradual reduction if overweight; target BMI 18.5-24.9
Dietary modification: consider Mediterranean diet or low-inflammatory approach
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If inadequate response to combination topical therapy within 6-8 weeks
For phototherapy assessment if topical therapy alone insufficient
For evaluation of systemic involvement: arthropathy, nail disease, metabolic syndrome
warnings: Potent topical corticosteroid: risk of HPA axis suppression with extensive use; limit duration
Phototherapy: increased melanoma risk with prolonged cumulative UVB exposure; monitor for new lesions

immediate_care: URGENT: Dermatology/rheumatology consultation for systemic immunosuppression assessment
Consider hospitalization if erythrodermic or pustular psoriasis (sepsis/dehydration risk)
Initiate biologic therapy or systemic retinoid depending on clinical subtype
medications: Biologic agent PREFERRED: TNF-alpha inhibitor (adalimumab 40mg q2 weeks) or IL-17 inhibitor (secukinumab 300mg weekly x4, then 300mg q4 weeks)
Alternative: IL-23 inhibitor (risankizumab 150mg SC x2 doses 4 weeks apart, then q8 weeks)
Systemic retinoid: acitretin 0.5-1 mg/kg daily (highly effective but teratogenic; requires contraception)
Methotrexate: 15-25 mg weekly (with folic acid 1mg daily) if biologics contraindicated
Phototherapy: intensive narrowband UVB (3x weekly) or PUVA (psoralen + UVA) concurrently
environmental_precautions: Intensive stress management: professional counseling given significant psychological impact
Complete alcohol and tobacco cessation mandatory
Climate consideration: phototherapy/sunlight exposure accelerates clearing
Infection prevention: monitor closely; infections may precipitate pustular conversion
lifestyle_adjustments: Sleep: optimize sleep; sedating antihistamine or low-dose amitriptyline if insomnia
Occupation: consider job modification if work stress identified as trigger
Dietary: comprehensive elimination diet; dairy, wheat, nightshades trial elimination
Exercise: regular moderate-intensity aerobic activity; weight management critical
when_to_seek_medical_attention: MANDATORY: Dermatology follow-up q4-6 weeks during biologic titration phase
EMERGENCY if fever, widespread erythema, or pustular conversion (systemic complications risk)
For monitoring of metabolic syndrome (psoriasis-associated CVD risk)
warnings: Biologic therapy: increased infection risk; monitor for TB, opportunistic infections
Acitretin: absolute teratogen; pregnancy must be absolutely prevented; 2-year washout required
TNF-alpha inhibitors: increased lymphoma risk; long-term surveillance mandatory

Herpes Simplex.description: Herpes simplex virus (HSV-1/-2) - recurrent viral infection with vesicular eruption

immediate_care: Initiate topical antiviral within 24-48 hours of prodromal symptoms for optimal efficacy
Apply acyclovir 5% cream or penciclovir 1% cream to affected area 5-6 times daily
Systemic antiviral indicated ONLY if first episode (acyclovir 400mg PO 5x daily x 7-10 days)
medications: First episode: acyclovir 400-800mg orally 5x daily x 7-10 days, or valacyclovir 1000mg TID x 7-10 days
Recurrent: topical acyclovir 5% or penciclovir 1% cream 5-6x daily x 4-5 days
Analgesia: acetaminophen 650mg or ibuprofen 400mg q4-6h for vesicle pain
environmental_precautions: Avoid contact with mucous membranes or abraded skin of others (highly contagious)
Do not share razors, towels, lip balms, or eating utensils during active infection
Maintain meticulous hand hygiene; avoid autoinoculation to other body areas
lifestyle_adjustments: Rest: allow adequate recovery time; fatigue exacerbates viral replication
Stress management: psychological stress major trigger for recurrence
Sleep optimization: 7-9 hours nightly supports immune function
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If lesions do not heal within 10-14 days; consider systemic antiviral escalation
For frequent recurrences (>6 per year); consider suppressive therapy
warnings: First episode typically more severe; may have systemic symptoms (fever, malaise, lymphadenopathy)

immediate_care: Initiate systemic antiviral immediately if recurrent episode or first episode
Acyclovir 400mg PO 5x daily or valacyclovir 1000mg TID for 7-10 days
Apply topical anesthetic (benzocaine 20%) if pain severely compromises function
medications: Valacyclovir 1000mg TID x 10 days (better bioavailability than acyclovir)
Alternative: acyclovir 800mg 5x daily x 7-10 days
Pain management: topical benzocaine 20%, oral acetaminophen 650mg, or ibuprofen 400mg q4-6h
environmental_precautions: Complete contagion precautions: avoid direct contact with lesions
For genital herpes: abstain from sexual contact during active infection
Hygiene: separate hand towel; meticulous hand hygiene after lesion contact
lifestyle_adjustments: Trigger avoidance: identify personal triggers (stress, menstrual cycle, sexual intercourse)
Stress reduction: meditation, yoga, psychotherapy if frequent triggers identified
Sleep: prioritize 7-9 hours nightly during acute episode and regularly
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If lesions persist beyond 14 days despite antiviral therapy
For suppressive therapy assessment if recurrences frequent or severe
warnings: Antiviral efficacy highest within 24-48 hours of symptom onset; early treatment critical
Secondary bacterial infection risk if lesions scratched; avoid manipulation

immediate_care: Consider hospitalization if extensive cutaneous involvement (erythema multiforme risk) or systemic symptoms
IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h if disseminated cutaneous disease or CNS involvement suspected
Ophthalmology consultation if ocular involvement (keratitis risk)
medications: Hospitalization: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h x 7-14 days for disseminated disease
Outpatient: valacyclovir 1000mg PO TID x 10 days or acyclovir 800mg PO 5x daily x 10 days
Pain management: tramadol 50-100mg q4-6h or morphine if severe neuropathic pain
Suppressive therapy: valacyclovir 500mg daily indefinitely to prevent recurrences
environmental_precautions: Strict contact precautions: avoid all direct contact with lesions
For genital herpes: absolute sexual abstinence during active infection; disclosure to partners recommended
Healthcare worker considerations: temporary reassignment if extensive hand/finger involvement
lifestyle_adjustments: Comprehensive stress reduction: professional psychological support if stress-triggered
Sleep optimization: consider low-dose sedating antidepressant (amitriptyline 10-25mg) if insomnia present
Lifestyle modification: identify and eliminate all identified triggers
when_to_seek_medical_attention: URGENT: if fever, meningismus, or neurological symptoms (CNS involvement)
For ophthalmology consultation if eye involvement
Frequent specialist follow-up if disseminated disease or complications
warnings: Disseminated cutaneous HSV carries risk of erythema multiforme or visceral involvement
IV acyclovir required for severe/disseminated disease; significant CNS penetration essential
Long-term suppressive therapy may be necessary for frequent recurrences

Vitiligo.description: Vitiligo - acquired depigmentation disorder due to melanocyte loss (autoimmune etiology)

immediate_care: Initiate potent topical corticosteroid: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment once daily
Apply to depigmented patches only; avoid surrounding normal skin
Use continuous application for 2-3 months, then assess response
medications: Topical corticosteroid: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment once daily to depigmented areas
Consider topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment as steroid-sparing alternative
Combine with topical vitamin D analogue (calcitriol 3 mcg/g) for potential synergy
environmental_precautions: Strict photoprotection: SPF 50+ sunscreen to depigmented areas (risk of severe sunburn)
Physical barriers: sun-protective clothing for affected areas, especially face/neck
Cosmetic camouflage: DermaCatch, Microskin, or full-coverage foundations for cosmetic concerns
lifestyle_adjustments: Thyroid function assessment: test TSH annually (autoimmune thyroid disease associated in 30%)
Vitamin B12 assessment: supplementation if deficient (pernicious anemia associated)
Sun exposure: avoid unnecessary exposure; depigmented areas at severe sunburn risk
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If inadequate response to topical therapy within 3-4 months
For phototherapy assessment (narrowband UVB) if extensive or progressive
warnings: Topical corticosteroid: tachyphylaxis develops with prolonged use; cyclical application recommended
Photosensitivity significant in depigmented areas; aggressive sun protection essential

immediate_care: Initiate combination therapy: potent topical corticosteroid + topical calcineurin inhibitor + phototherapy
Consider narrowband UVB phototherapy (311 nm) 2-3x weekly
Topical corticosteroid: mometasone 0.1% or clobetasol 0.05% daily to patches
medications: Topical corticosteroid: mometasone furoate 0.1% cream once daily
Topical calcineurin inhibitor: tacrolimus 0.1% ointment once daily as steroid-sparing agent
Vitamin D analogue: calcipotriene 0.005% ointment once daily for additive effect
Phototherapy: narrowband UVB 311 nm, 2-3x weekly; excellent response rates with combination therapy
environmental_precautions: Obsessive photoprotection: SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen q2h to depigmented areas
Protective clothing: wide-brimmed hat, long sleeves, UV-protective fabrics
Cosmetic: consider professional application of full-coverage foundations (Dermablend, Microskin)
lifestyle_adjustments: Comprehensive thyroid assessment: TSH, free T4, TPO antibodies annually
Nutritional assessment: B12, folate, iron levels; supplement if deficient
Psychosocial support: depression/anxiety common given cosmetic concerns; consider counseling
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If inadequate response to combination therapy within 4-6 months
For systemic therapy assessment (JAK inhibitors emerging data) if extensive involvement
Ophthalmology referral: assess for ocular involvement (rare but possible)
warnings: Phototherapy: ensure baseline ophthalmology exam; monitor for cataracts with prolonged UVB exposure
Systemic manifestations: monitor for autoimmune thyroid disease, pernicious anemia

immediate_care: Consider systemic JAK inhibitor therapy (ruxolitinib) - emerging evidence for efficacy
Intensive phototherapy: narrowband UVB 3x weekly + topical combination therapy
Assess for depigmentation affecting >30% BSA; consider depigmentation therapy alternative if >50% BSA
medications: JAK inhibitor: ruxolitinib 15-20% cream topical (FDA-approved 2022); apply daily to patches
Systemic JAK inhibitor: baricitinib 2-4mg daily or tofacitinib 5mg BID if extensive (emerging evidence)
Phototherapy: narrowband UVB 311 nm, 3x weekly + excimer laser (308 nm) to individual patches
Topical: clobetasol 0.05% ointment + tacrolimus 0.1% + vitamin D analogue combination
environmental_precautions: Complete cosmetic management: professional full-coverage foundation application or tattooing
Protective clothing: cover exposed depigmented areas; UV-protective fabrics essential
Cosmetic tattooing: consider intradermal pigmentation for periorbital/lip vitiligo
lifestyle_adjustments: Intensive psychosocial support: depression/body dysmorphia common; professional counseling recommended
Comprehensive metabolic workup: thyroid, B12, folate, iron; supplement aggressively if deficient
Support groups: vitiligo support organizations provide psychological peer support
when_to_seek_medical_attention: MANDATORY: Systemic therapy assessment if extensive (>30% BSA) or rapidly progressive
Regular phototherapy monitoring: ophthalmology exam q6-12 months
Dermatology surveillance: evaluate treatment response q2-3 months
warnings: JAK inhibitors: increased infection risk; monitor for TB, opportunistic infections
Phototherapy: cumulative UVB exposure increases melanoma risk; lifetime monitoring essential
Extensive vitiligo carries psychological morbidity; mental health support non-negotiable

Basal Cell Carcinoma.description: Most common skin cancer; locally invasive, rarely metastasises

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology referral within 1–2 weeks for biopsy confirmation
Photograph and document lesion borders, size, and colour changes
Avoid sun exposure to the affected site; cover with breathable dressing
medications: Topical imiquimod 5% cream (for superficial BCC) — apply 5 days/week for 6 weeks
Topical fluorouracil 5% cream as alternative for superficial lesions
No OTC medications substitute for surgical or specialist treatment
environmental_precautions: Strict photoprotection: broad-spectrum SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen, reapply every 2 hours
Wear UPF 50+ protective clothing and wide-brim hat outdoors
Avoid peak UV hours (10 AM – 4 PM); seek shade whenever possible
lifestyle_adjustments: Self-examination monthly for new or changing lesions
Annual full-body skin checks with a dermatologist
Avoid tanning beds completely; they significantly increase BCC risk
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Immediately if lesion bleeds spontaneously or grows rapidly
If a pearly, translucent, or ulcerated lesion appears anywhere on the body
Do not delay dermatology referral — early excision is curative
warnings: BCC is a malignancy — do not treat with home remedies or delay specialist review
Immunosuppressed patients have higher recurrence rates; vigilant monitoring essential

immediate_care: Expedite dermatology referral within days; surgical excision likely indicated
Avoid trauma to lesion; keep area clean and covered
Document any recent growth, colour change, or new satellite lesions
medications: Post-excision: topical antibiotic ointment (mupirocin 2%) to surgical site
Vismodegib (Hedgehog pathway inhibitor) for locally advanced cases — specialist only
Adequate analgesia post-procedure as directed by surgeon
environmental_precautions: Strict sun avoidance and SPF 50+ application daily
Avoid chemical irritants and fragranced products around lesion site
Air-conditioned environments recommended during high UV index days
lifestyle_adjustments: Smoking cessation — tobacco impairs wound healing post-excision
Balanced diet rich in antioxidants (vitamins C, E, beta-carotene)
Psychological support may be beneficial given cancer diagnosis
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Immediately if signs of local spread (satellite nodules, lymph node swelling)
If wound post-excision shows signs of infection (redness, pus, fever)
Follow scheduled dermatology reviews without delay
warnings: Moderate BCC requires surgical excision — topical therapy alone insufficient
High recurrence zones: nose, ears, lips, eyelids — require Mohs surgery

immediate_care: Emergency referral to dermatological oncology within 24–48 hours
Multidisciplinary team (MDT) assessment for surgical planning
CT/MRI imaging if perineural or bone invasion suspected
medications: Vismodegib 150mg daily or sonidegib 200mg daily (Hedgehog inhibitors) for inoperable BCC
Cemiplimab (PD-1 inhibitor) for locally advanced or metastatic BCC — oncologist directed
Systemic analgesia for pain management as prescribed
environmental_precautions: Complete avoidance of UV exposure; indoor sun protection still required (window UV)
Environmental triggers of immunosuppression to be minimised
Wound care and infection prevention protocols post-surgery
lifestyle_adjustments: Nutritional optimisation to support surgical recovery and immune function
Multidisciplinary psychological and oncology support
Strict smoking and alcohol cessation
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any new neurological symptoms (numbness, facial weakness) — perineural spread
Systemic symptoms: unexplained weight loss, fatigue — rare metastasis
All follow-up appointments are mandatory
warnings: Severe BCC may require Mohs micrographic surgery, radiation, or systemic therapy
Metastatic BCC is rare but life-threatening; immediate oncology involvement essential

Benign tumors.description: Non-cancerous skin growths including lipomas, fibromas, and sebaceous cysts

immediate_care: Observe and monitor lesion size, shape, and colour over time
Photograph lesion monthly to track any changes
Dermatology review if uncertain about benign nature
medications: No pharmacological treatment typically required for benign growths
Topical retinoids may reduce sebaceous hyperplasia if cosmetically bothersome
Intralesional corticosteroid injection for symptomatic lipomas — specialist only
environmental_precautions: Protect lesion from friction, trauma, and repeated pressure
Use SPF 30+ if lesion is sun-exposed to prevent UV-induced changes
Keep area clean and dry to prevent secondary infection
lifestyle_adjustments: Regular skin self-examination to detect any change in existing growths
Avoid squeezing or manipulating growths — risk of infection
Annual dermatology review for monitoring
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If lesion rapidly increases in size, changes colour, or bleeds spontaneously
If uncertain whether growth is benign — biopsy provides definitive diagnosis
If causing pain, functional limitation, or significant cosmetic distress

immediate_care: Schedule dermatology consultation for formal assessment and possible excision
Avoid self-removal attempts — risk of infection and scarring
Document all symptomatic changes with photographs
medications: Post-excision wound care: topical antibiotic ointment and non-adherent dressing
NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400mg) for post-procedural discomfort
Silicone gel sheeting post-excision to minimise scarring
environmental_precautions: Protect surgical site from sun exposure during healing (SPF 50+ or physical cover)
Avoid swimming pools or open water until wound fully healed
lifestyle_adjustments: Keep surgical site elevated if on extremity to reduce swelling
Maintain optimal nutrition (protein, vitamin C) to support wound healing
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Signs of wound infection: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, or discharge
Suture concerns or wound dehiscence post-excision
warnings: Some benign tumours (e.g. atypical lipoma) have malignant potential — histopathology required

immediate_care: Urgent surgical evaluation for rapidly enlarging or symptomatic growths
Imaging (ultrasound or MRI) to assess depth and vascular involvement
MDT assessment if malignant transformation suspected
medications: Specialist-directed pharmacotherapy post-surgical intervention
Adequate post-operative analgesia and wound management
environmental_precautions: Full post-operative wound protection and infection prevention
lifestyle_adjustments: Post-surgical rehabilitation as directed by surgical team
Oncology referral if histopathology reveals unexpected malignancy
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Emergency care if growth causes neurovascular compromise
Immediate review if histopathology reveals atypical or malignant features
warnings: Large or deep benign tumours may require general anaesthesia for excision
Histopathological analysis of all excised tissue is mandatory

Bullous.description: Blistering skin disorders including bullous pemphigoid and pemphigus vulgaris

immediate_care: Do not rupture blisters — intact blisters protect against infection
Cover with non-adherent sterile dressing; change daily
Urgent dermatology referral — bullous conditions require specialist management
medications: Topical high-potency corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05%) for localised bullous pemphigoid
Mild antiseptic wash (dilute chlorhexidine) for erosions after blister rupture
Antihistamines for itch: cetirizine 10mg once daily
environmental_precautions: Avoid friction, heat, and pressure on affected skin
Use loose, soft clothing (100% cotton) to minimise trauma
Maintain cool indoor environment — heat worsens blistering
lifestyle_adjustments: Soft, non-irritating diet if oral mucosa involved
Avoid NSAIDs and ACE inhibitors — can trigger bullous eruptions
Maintain meticulous wound hygiene to prevent secondary bacterial infection
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Immediately if blisters spread rapidly, involve mucous membranes, or cause fever
If unable to eat or drink due to oral involvement
Urgent review if secondary bacterial infection suspected (pus, odour, fever)
warnings: Bullous disorders carry infection and fluid loss risk — do not delay specialist review
Immunosuppressive therapy is often required — self-treatment is insufficient

immediate_care: Hospital-based dermatology review within 24–48 hours
Wound care by specialist nursing team for widespread erosions
Nutritional assessment if oral intake is compromised
medications: Oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day for active bullous pemphigoid
Doxycycline 100mg twice daily + nicotinamide 500mg three times daily (steroid-sparing)
Azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil as steroid-sparing agents — specialist only
environmental_precautions: Hospital-grade wound dressings; avoid adhesive tapes directly on skin
Temperature-controlled environment; avoid excessive sweating
lifestyle_adjustments: High-protein diet to support wound healing and counter corticosteroid catabolism
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation with systemic steroids
Psychological support — chronic blistering diseases significantly impact quality of life
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Worsening despite oral steroids — may need IV therapy or biologics
Signs of sepsis: high fever, confusion, rapid heart rate
warnings: Systemic corticosteroids carry significant side effects — bone protection mandatory
Pemphigus vulgaris is potentially life-threatening — never delay treatment

immediate_care: Emergency hospital admission for widespread blistering or sepsis risk
IV fluid and electrolyte management for extensive skin loss
Intensive wound care in specialised burns/dermatology unit
medications: IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy for rapidly progressive disease
Rituximab (anti-CD20) for refractory pemphigus vulgaris — specialist only
IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) for severe refractory cases
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics if secondary sepsis develops
environmental_precautions: Sterile environment; infection control precautions in inpatient setting
Non-adherent wound dressings changed by trained nursing staff
lifestyle_adjustments: Parenteral nutrition if enteral intake severely compromised
MDT approach: dermatology, nutritional support, ophthalmology (if ocular involvement)
when_to_seek_medical_attention: This severity requires emergency hospital care — do not manage at home
ICU-level monitoring if >30% body surface area affected
warnings: Severe bullous disease mortality risk is significant — emergency specialist care only
All immunosuppressants require infection screening (TB, hepatitis, HIV) before initiation

Candidiasis.description: Fungal infection caused by Candida species; affects skin folds, mucosa, and nails

immediate_care: Keep affected area clean and thoroughly dry — moisture promotes Candida growth
Wear breathable cotton underwear and loose clothing
Apply topical antifungal cream to affected area twice daily
medications: Clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 2–4 weeks
Miconazole 2% cream or powder as alternative, especially for intertriginous areas
Nystatin cream for oral or diaper candidiasis in infants
environmental_precautions: Avoid occlusive clothing; maximise skin ventilation
Change damp or sweaty clothing immediately
Avoid scented soaps and bath products in affected areas
lifestyle_adjustments: Reduce dietary refined sugar and alcohol — both promote Candida overgrowth
Control blood glucose if diabetic — hyperglycaemia is a major risk factor
Use barrier cream (zinc oxide) in skin folds to reduce moisture
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If symptoms persist beyond 2 weeks of topical antifungal therapy
If infection spreads or new satellite lesions appear
If recurrent — may indicate underlying immunosuppression or uncontrolled diabetes
warnings: Recurrent candidiasis may indicate HIV, diabetes, or immune deficiency — investigate
Avoid topical steroids alone — they worsen candidal infections

immediate_care: Dermatology or GP review for systemic antifungal consideration
Intensive skin fold hygiene; keep dry with absorbent powder
Treat any contributing factors: glycaemic control, antibiotic reduction
medications: Oral fluconazole 150mg single dose (or 150mg weekly for recurrent episodes)
Itraconazole 100–200mg daily for 2 weeks for resistant cases
Continue topical clotrimazole alongside oral therapy for skin candidiasis
environmental_precautions: Avoid communal pools, gyms, and humid environments during active infection
Disinfect clothing and towels at >60°C wash cycle
lifestyle_adjustments: Probiotics (Lactobacillus species) may reduce recurrence in vulvovaginal candidiasis
Low-glycaemic diet to reduce systemic yeast load
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If oral candidiasis (thrush) persists — HIV or haematological malignancy exclusion needed
Worsening despite oral fluconazole — consider fluconazole-resistant Candida (C. krusei, C. glabrata)
warnings: Fluconazole is teratogenic — avoid in pregnancy; use topical azoles instead
Drug interaction: fluconazole potentiates warfarin — monitor INR closely

immediate_care: Urgent medical review for invasive or widespread cutaneous candidiasis
Blood cultures and fungal serology if systemic involvement suspected
Hospital admission for immunocompromised patients with severe candidiasis
medications: IV echinocandin (caspofungin, micafungin) for suspected invasive candidiasis
IV fluconazole or voriconazole if Candida albicans confirmed and patient stable
Antifungal therapy duration: minimum 14 days after first negative culture
environmental_precautions: Strict infection control in hospitalised patients
Central line management if catheter-related candidaemia suspected
lifestyle_adjustments: Immunosuppression reduction where clinically feasible
Nutritional optimisation; correct underlying metabolic abnormalities
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Candidaemia (Candida in bloodstream) is a medical emergency — hospital care only
Ophthalmology review for ocular candidiasis in all candidaemia patients
warnings: Invasive candidiasis carries 30–40% mortality — aggressive treatment mandatory
Remove central venous catheters as soon as candidaemia confirmed

Dermatofibroma.description: Common benign fibrous nodule typically on lower limbs

immediate_care: No treatment necessary for asymptomatic dermatofibromas
Reassure patient of benign nature with characteristic dimple sign
Dermatology review if atypical features present (rapid growth, ulceration)
medications: No pharmacological treatment required for typical dermatofibromas
Intralesional corticosteroid (triamcinolone 10mg/mL) if symptomatic or pruritic
Topical antipruritic (hydrocortisone 1%) for occasional itch
environmental_precautions: Protect lesion from razor nicks during shaving — common site is lower leg
Apply SPF if sun-exposed to prevent pigmentation changes
lifestyle_adjustments: No lifestyle modification required
Annual skin check recommended to monitor for any atypical changes
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If lesion grows rapidly, becomes painful, or ulcerates
If multiple dermatofibromas appear suddenly — may indicate systemic condition
If diagnosis is uncertain — dermoscopy or biopsy provides clarity
warnings: Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP) can mimic dermatofibroma — biopsy if any doubt

immediate_care: Dermatology consultation for symptomatic or cosmetically significant lesions
Surgical excision if causing pain or functional limitation
medications: Post-excision: topical antibiotic ointment (mupirocin 2%) for wound care
Silicone gel post-healing to optimise scar outcome
environmental_precautions: Post-excision sun protection to prevent hyperpigmented scarring
Avoid pressure or trauma to surgical site during healing
lifestyle_adjustments: Monitor scar healing; massage with emollient once wound closed
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Post-operative signs of infection or wound breakdown
Recurrence at excision site — unusual for benign dermatofibroma

immediate_care: Urgent biopsy if rapidly enlarging — exclude dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans
Imaging to assess depth and involvement of deeper tissues
medications: Imatinib (Gleevec) for unresectable DFSP if confirmed — oncologist only
environmental_precautions: Post-surgical care and infection prevention as directed by surgical team
lifestyle_adjustments: MDT follow-up including oncology if DFSP confirmed
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Immediate oncology referral if histopathology reveals DFSP or atypical features
warnings: DFSP has high local recurrence rate — wide local excision or Mohs surgery required

DrugEruption.description: Cutaneous adverse drug reaction ranging from maculopapular rash to severe reactions (SJS/TEN)

immediate_care: Identify and discontinue the suspected causative drug immediately
Document the rash onset timing relative to drug initiation
Report to prescribing physician or pharmacist urgently
medications: Oral antihistamine: cetirizine 10mg or loratadine 10mg once daily for pruritus
Topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%) for localised erythematous areas
Calamine lotion for symptomatic relief of itch and irritation
environmental_precautions: Avoid all forms of the suspected drug and structurally related medications
Inform all healthcare providers of the drug reaction for medical records
Wear medical alert bracelet if severe reaction history
lifestyle_adjustments: Maintain drug allergy record and share with all prescribers
Avoid self-medicating with OTC products containing the implicated drug class
Stay well-hydrated to support drug elimination
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Rash spreads rapidly, involves mucous membranes, or causes blistering — EMERGENCY
If fever, lymph node swelling, or facial oedema develops (DRESS syndrome)
If rash does not resolve within 1–2 weeks of stopping the causative drug
warnings: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN) are life-threatening — any mucosal involvement requires emergency care
Do NOT rechallenge with the causative drug without specialist supervision

immediate_care: Emergency medical review — moderate drug eruptions carry escalation risk
Stop causative drug immediately; document in permanent medical record
Ophthalmology review if ocular involvement suspected
medications: Oral prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day for significant maculopapular or urticarial eruption
Systemic antihistamines for itch and urticaria
Topical emollients for barrier restoration on affected skin
environmental_precautions: Avoid heat and sun — both can worsen drug-induced rash
Cool, loose cotton clothing to minimise irritation
lifestyle_adjustments: Rest and adequate hydration
Nutritional support if appetite reduced due to mucosal involvement
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any mucosal blistering or skin detachment — immediate emergency department
Systemic symptoms: jaundice, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia — DRESS evaluation
warnings: DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) has 10% mortality
Causative drug must be permanently avoided and documented

immediate_care: Emergency hospital admission — SJS/TEN requires ICU-level care
Stop all non-essential medications immediately
IV fluid resuscitation for extensive skin detachment
medications: Cyclosporine or IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) for SJS/TEN — specialist burns/dermatology unit
TNF inhibitors (etanercept, infliximab) emerging evidence for TEN management
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics for secondary sepsis prevention
Systemic corticosteroids are CONTROVERSIAL in SJS/TEN — specialist decision only
environmental_precautions: Sterile wound management in burns unit or specialised dermatology ward
Ophthalmological care to prevent corneal scarring
lifestyle_adjustments: MDT: dermatology, ophthalmology, intensive care, plastic surgery
Psychological trauma support post-recovery
when_to_seek_medical_attention: SJS/TEN = dermatological emergency — immediate hospital admission, do not delay
warnings: TEN mortality rate: 30–50% — requires specialised burns/dermatology unit management
All implicated drugs must be permanently blacklisted; rechallenge can be fatal

Hailey-Hailey Disease.description: Rare hereditary blistering disorder (familial benign chronic pemphigus) affecting skin folds

immediate_care: Keep affected skin folds clean and completely dry — moisture triggers flares
Apply topical corticosteroid (mometasone 0.1%) to reduce inflammation
Avoid friction in affected areas; use soft, breathable cotton clothing
medications: Topical corticosteroid (betamethasone 0.05% or mometasone 0.1%) twice daily during flares
Topical antifungal + antibacterial combination (clotrimazole + neomycin) for secondary infections
Topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment as steroid-sparing maintenance therapy
environmental_precautions: Minimise sweating — use fans, air conditioning, and avoid exertion in heat
Avoid tight clothing, elastic waistbands, and synthetic fabrics
Keep skin folds dry with talc-free absorbent powders
lifestyle_adjustments: Identify and avoid personal triggers: friction, heat, sweating, infection, stress
Weight management reduces skin fold friction — a key precipitating factor
Apply zinc oxide paste to skin folds as barrier protection
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If secondary bacterial or fungal infection develops (pus, odour, spreading redness)
If flares occur more than 3–4 times per year — consider preventive therapy
Annual dermatology review for disease monitoring and treatment optimisation
warnings: Hailey-Hailey Disease is lifelong — complete cure is not possible; management focuses on flare control
Herpes simplex co-infection (Kaposi varicelliform eruption) can cause severe flares — antiviral treatment needed

immediate_care: Dermatology review within 1–2 weeks for refractory or widespread flares
Swab erosions for bacterial and fungal culture to guide antibiotic/antifungal choice
Commence systemic antibiotic if secondary bacterial infection confirmed
medications: Oral doxycycline 100mg twice daily for secondary bacterial infection control
Oral fluconazole 150mg weekly if Candida co-infection confirmed
Oral aciclovir 400mg three times daily if herpes simplex co-infection suspected
Topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.025%) applied to lesions — reduces keratinocyte cohesion defect
Botulinum toxin A injections into affected axillae/groins — reduces sweating, prevents flares
environmental_precautions: Avoid hot weather, saunas, and excessive physical activity during flares
Use portable fans and cooling vests in hot climates
Daily showering with gentle antibacterial wash (chlorhexidine 2%) during flares
lifestyle_adjustments: Psychosocial support — chronic flares in skin folds significantly affect quality of life
Stress management through CBT or mindfulness — stress is a known flare trigger
Dietary anti-inflammatory foods may have modest benefit
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If widespread erosions, fever, or systemic illness develops
If condition significantly impacts work, mobility, or mental health
Botulinum toxin referral if sweating is the primary trigger
warnings: Prolonged topical corticosteroid use in skin folds causes atrophy — limit duration and potency
Genetic counselling recommended — autosomal dominant inheritance (50% transmission risk)

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology inpatient assessment for extensive or superinfected disease
Wound care by specialist nursing team for widespread erosions
IV antibiotics if severe secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis or bacteraemia)
medications: Oral retinoids (acitretin 0.5mg/kg/day) for refractory extensive disease
Dapsone 50–100mg daily as second-line systemic option
Cyclosporine or methotrexate for severe refractory cases — specialist supervised
Nd:YAG or CO2 laser therapy — effective for recalcitrant lesions in specialist centres
Photodynamic therapy (PDT) as emerging option for localised severe disease
environmental_precautions: Climate-controlled living environment essential — air conditioning reduces flare frequency
Hospital-grade wound dressings for extensive erosions
Avoid all known personal triggers rigorously
lifestyle_adjustments: Consider surgical management (dermabrasion, excision with skin grafting) for severely affected sites
Multidisciplinary approach: dermatology, clinical psychology, genetics, nutritional support
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Urgent admission if fever, spreading cellulitis, or bacteraemia suspected
Immediate review if oral retinoid side effects develop (mucocutaneous toxicity, hypertriglyceridaemia)
warnings: Oral retinoids (acitretin) are teratogenic — mandatory contraception for 3 years post-treatment in women of childbearing age
Severe Hailey-Hailey significantly impairs quality of life — psychological support is non-negotiable

Impetigo.description: Highly contagious superficial bacterial skin infection caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes

immediate_care: Gently clean lesions with antiseptic solution (chlorhexidine 0.05%) twice daily
Apply topical antibiotic cream directly to crusted lesions
Keep nails short to prevent auto-inoculation by scratching
medications: Topical mupirocin 2% ointment three times daily for 5–7 days (first-line for localised)
Topical retapamulin 1% ointment as alternative for mupirocin-resistant cases
Topical fusidic acid 2% cream twice daily for 7 days
environmental_precautions: Isolate affected individual from school/daycare until 48 hours of antibiotic therapy completed
Do not share towels, flannels, or clothing
Wash all items at >60°C; disinfect surfaces in contact with lesions
lifestyle_adjustments: Wash hands thoroughly after touching lesions
Avoid direct contact sports until lesions fully crusted or healed
Change bed linen daily during active infection
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If lesions spread rapidly or new crops appear after 48 hours of treatment
If child develops fever or appears systemically unwell
If lesions involve periorbital area or don't respond to topical therapy
warnings: Impetigo is highly contagious — isolate patient and educate household contacts
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is a rare but serious complication — monitor urine if streptococcal cause confirmed

immediate_care: Oral antibiotic initiation after clinical assessment
Warm compress application to soften and remove crusts before antibiotic application
GP or dermatology review to exclude bullous impetigo
medications: Oral flucloxacillin 500mg four times daily for 7 days (Staphylococcal coverage)
Oral cefalexin 500mg twice daily as alternative
Erythromycin 500mg twice daily if penicillin allergy
MRSA-directed therapy (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) if MRSA confirmed
environmental_precautions: Enhanced household hygiene; all contacts should use antiseptic handwash
Avoid public swimming pools for 1 week post-resolution
lifestyle_adjustments: Screen household contacts for asymptomatic carriage if recurrent episodes
Consider mupirocin nasal decolonisation for recurrent Staphylococcal impetigo
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Failure to respond to oral antibiotics within 48–72 hours
Spreading cellulitis or signs of systemic infection
warnings: Recurrent impetigo warrants nasal swab culture to identify Staphylococcal carriage

immediate_care: Hospital assessment for widespread or ecthymatous (deep) impetigo
IV antibiotics if systemically unwell or unable to tolerate oral therapy
Wound swab culture to guide antibiotic selection
medications: IV flucloxacillin 1–2g four times daily for severe infection
IV vancomycin for confirmed or suspected MRSA
Step-down to oral antibiotics once clinically stable
environmental_precautions: Contact precautions in hospital settings
Strict isolation from other patients
lifestyle_adjustments: Immunodeficiency workup if severe or recurrent impetigo in adult
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Immediately if fever, lymphangitis, or bacteraemia signs present
Urgent if periorbital involvement threatening orbital cellulitis
warnings: Ecthyma (deep impetigo) can cause permanent scarring — early aggressive treatment essential

Infestations Bites.description: Skin reactions to insect bites, scabies, lice, or other ectoparasitic infestations

immediate_care: Wash the affected area with soap and water
Apply cold compress to reduce swelling and itch
Remove any visible stinger (bee) with horizontal scraping — do not squeeze
medications: Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily for localised pruritus and inflammation
Oral antihistamine: cetirizine 10mg or loratadine 10mg for itch and urticaria
Calamine lotion for cooling relief of multiple bite sites
environmental_precautions: Apply DEET-based repellent (20–30%) before outdoor exposure
Wear long sleeves and trousers in high-insect-density environments
Use permethrin-treated clothing and bed nets in endemic areas
lifestyle_adjustments: Avoid scratching — risk of secondary bacterial infection
Treat all household members simultaneously for scabies and lice
Wash all clothing and bedding at high temperature for ectoparasitic infestations
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Signs of anaphylaxis: throat swelling, breathing difficulty, dizziness — EMERGENCY 999
If rash spreads, persists beyond 1 week, or shows signs of infection
If scabies or lice confirmed — prescription treatment required
warnings: Anaphylaxis from insect stings can be life-threatening — carry adrenaline autoinjector if known allergy
Tick bites in endemic areas: monitor for Lyme disease symptoms for 4 weeks

immediate_care: Medical review for suspected scabies, lice, or widespread infestation
Skin scraping or dermoscopy for scabies mite confirmation
Treat all household and close contacts simultaneously
medications: Permethrin 5% cream (scabies): apply from neck down, leave 8–12 hours, repeat after 1 week
Malathion 0.5% lotion (lice): apply to scalp, leave 12 hours, repeat after 7 days
Oral ivermectin 200mcg/kg (scabies) for crusted or refractory cases — GP prescription
Oral antihistamines and topical steroids for post-scabetic itch (can persist 4–6 weeks)
environmental_precautions: Machine wash all clothing and bedding at >60°C on treatment day
Items that cannot be washed: seal in plastic bag for 72 hours
Vacuum furniture and carpets thoroughly
lifestyle_adjustments: Avoid close physical contact until treatment completed
Sexual partners require simultaneous treatment for scabies
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If itch persists beyond 6 weeks post-treatment (Norwegian/crusted scabies)
Secondary bacterial infection of excoriation sites (spreading redness, pus)
warnings: Post-scabetic itch is NOT reinfestation — continue antipruritic therapy; avoid repeat permethrin overuse
Crusted (Norwegian) scabies is highly contagious and requires hospital-based management

immediate_care: Hospital review for crusted (Norwegian) scabies or severe hypersensitivity reaction
Anaphylaxis: IV adrenaline, antihistamine, and corticosteroid — emergency department
Infection control isolation for crusted scabies in healthcare settings
medications: Oral ivermectin repeat doses for crusted scabies + topical permethrin combination
IV adrenaline 0.5mg IM for anaphylaxis; IV hydrocortisone 200mg
IV antibiotics for secondary cellulitis complicating infestations
environmental_precautions: Full environmental decontamination for crusted scabies — specialist infection control
Contact precautions for all healthcare workers managing crusted scabies
lifestyle_adjustments: Nutritional support and skin barrier restoration post-infestation
Psychological support for distress related to infestation diagnosis
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Anaphylaxis symptoms — call emergency services immediately (999/112)
Hospital admission for crusted scabies, severe secondary cellulitis
warnings: Crusted scabies carries millions of mites — infection risk to contacts is extremely high
Anaphylaxis prescription: ensure patient and caregivers trained in adrenaline autoinjector use

Keratosis.description: Benign or premalignant skin growths including seborrhoeic keratosis and actinic keratosis

immediate_care: Differentiate between seborrhoeic keratosis (benign) and actinic keratosis (premalignant) — dermatoscopy
Apply daily broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen to prevent new actinic keratoses
Monitor for changes in size, texture, or colour
medications: Topical fluorouracil 5% cream for actinic keratosis — apply twice daily for 2–4 weeks
Topical imiquimod 5% cream applied 3 times weekly for actinic keratosis field therapy
Topical diclofenac 3% gel for mild actinic keratosis — apply twice daily for 60–90 days
environmental_precautions: Strict photoprotection — actinic keratoses are directly UV-induced
Wear wide-brim hat and UPF 50+ clothing in outdoor settings
Avoid tanning beds completely
lifestyle_adjustments: Annual skin checks with dermatologist for patients with multiple actinic keratoses
Smoking cessation — tobacco contributes to immunosuppression and UV damage repair impairment
Vitamin D supplement if excessive sun avoidance is required
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Actinic keratosis that is tender, thickened, or indurated — potential squamous cell carcinoma
Seborrhoeic keratosis with sudden multiplication (sign of Leser-Trélat — may indicate internal malignancy)
Any lesion with irregular border, bleeding, or ulceration
warnings: Actinic keratoses are premalignant — approximately 1–10% progress to squamous cell carcinoma if untreated
Do not remove seborrhoeic keratoses with home remedies — risk of scarring and masking malignancy

immediate_care: Dermatology review for treatment of multiple actinic keratoses or suspicious lesions
Cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen) for discrete actinic keratoses — effective and quick
Photodynamic therapy (PDT) for field cancerisation with multiple lesions
medications: Topical ingenol mebutate gel (Picato) 0.015% for face/scalp AK — 3 consecutive days
Continuation of topical field therapy (fluorouracil or imiquimod) for widespread AK
Systemic acitretin in transplant recipients with multiple AKs — specialist only
environmental_precautions: All outdoor activities require maximum UV protection
Consider UV-blocking window film for vehicles and home in high sun-exposure individuals
lifestyle_adjustments: Chemoprevention: oral nicotinamide 500mg twice daily shown to reduce new AK in high-risk patients
Regular skin self-examination monthly between dermatology visits
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Post-treatment failure — lesion persists or new lesions develop rapidly
Rapidly enlarging or painful AK — urgent biopsy to exclude SCC
warnings: Organ transplant recipients have 100x higher risk of SCC — require 3–6 monthly dermatology reviews

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology referral for biopsy of suspicious or rapidly changing keratoses
Multidisciplinary skin cancer meeting if SCC transformation suspected
Imaging if regional lymphadenopathy present
medications: Oral capecitabine or systemic retinoids for chemoprevention in very high-risk patients
Cemiplimab (PD-1 inhibitor) for locally advanced or metastatic SCC arising from AK
environmental_precautions: Total sun abstinence; photosensitising medications review
Strict infection prevention post-surgical intervention
lifestyle_adjustments: Oncology MDT involvement for invasive SCC arising from AK field
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any confirmed SCC requires urgent surgical referral
Lymph node metastasis requires immediate oncology review
warnings: SCC arising in chronically immunosuppressed patients carries worse prognosis — aggressive treatment required

Larva Migrans.description: Cutaneous larva migrans — hookworm larval migration through skin, typically from contaminated sand/soil

immediate_care: Do not attempt to remove larvae by freezing or extraction — ineffective and harmful
Keep affected area clean and apply topical therapy to eliminate larvae
Avoid walking barefoot on beaches or soil in endemic tropical regions
medications: Topical thiabendazole 10–15% applied three times daily for 7–10 days
Oral ivermectin 200mcg/kg single dose (first-line oral therapy — highly effective)
Oral albendazole 400–800mg daily for 3–7 days as alternative to ivermectin
environmental_precautions: Wear sandals and protective footwear in endemic areas
Avoid lying directly on sand — use barrier mats or towels
Discourage cats and dogs from beach areas (they are the primary reservoir hosts)
lifestyle_adjustments: Report diagnosis — helps public health monitoring in travel clinics
Full resolution expected within days of treatment initiation
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection of excoriated tracks
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Widespread skin involvement or no response to treatment within 5 days
Secondary bacterial infection of larval tracks (redness, pus, fever)
Löffler syndrome (pulmonary symptoms) if larvae migrate beyond skin — rare
warnings: Cutaneous larva migrans is self-limiting but causes intense pruritus — treatment significantly accelerates resolution
Löffler's syndrome (eosinophilic pulmonary infiltration) is a rare complication

immediate_care: Oral antiparasitic therapy for widespread or treatment-resistant cutaneous larva migrans
Secondary bacterial infection management with topical or oral antibiotics if present
medications: Oral ivermectin 200mcg/kg repeated after 1 week for persistent cases
Oral cetirizine 10mg for symptomatic itch during treatment course
Topical mupirocin 2% if secondary bacterial superinfection present
environmental_precautions: Continue footwear in all high-risk environments
Educate travel companions about prevention measures
lifestyle_adjustments: Travel medicine follow-up after tropical travel with skin symptoms
Record exposure history for epidemiological purposes
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If eosinophilia detected on blood count (systemic larval migration risk)
Persistent pruritic serpiginous tracks after two courses of treatment

immediate_care: Specialist tropical medicine or dermatology review for systemic or treatment-refractory cases
Full blood count and eosinophil count to assess systemic involvement
medications: Combination therapy: oral ivermectin + albendazole for very resistant cases
Manage Löffler syndrome with corticosteroids if pulmonary eosinophilia confirmed
environmental_precautions: Stringent protective footwear and barrier measures in all future endemic area exposures
lifestyle_adjustments: Tropical medicine follow-up to rule out concurrent parasitic infections (strongyloides)
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Pulmonary symptoms (cough, wheeze, dyspnoea) after tropical exposure — immediate review
warnings: Visceral larva migrans from Toxocara (different organism) can affect eyes and organs — distinct condition requiring specialist care

Leprosy.description: Chronic infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium leprae affecting skin, nerves, and mucosa

immediate_care: Urgent referral to infectious disease or leprosy specialist for classification and MDT initiation
Skin biopsy and slit-skin smear for bacterial load assessment
Neurological assessment for peripheral nerve involvement
medications: WHO Multi-Drug Therapy (MDT) — Paucibacillary (PB): Dapsone 100mg daily + Rifampicin 600mg monthly for 6 months
Do NOT initiate treatment without specialist supervision — classification essential
Protective footwear for anaesthetic feet to prevent plantar ulceration
environmental_precautions: Leprosy is NOT highly contagious — household contacts require annual skin examination only
Wear protective gloves and footwear to prevent injury to anaesthetic extremities
Avoid extreme temperatures — impaired sensation increases burn/injury risk
lifestyle_adjustments: Daily skin and extremity inspection for insensate areas
Physiotherapy to maintain hand and foot function
Patient education about nerve damage prevention
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If sudden onset of nerve pain, weakness, or paralysis (lepra reaction) — URGENT
Plantar ulceration or unhealed wounds in anaesthetic feet
Eye involvement (lagophthalmos, reduced visual acuity) — ophthalmology urgently
warnings: Lepra reactions (Type 1 and Type 2 — erythema nodosum leprosum) are immune-mediated and can cause rapid permanent nerve damage
Leprosy carries significant social stigma — psychosocial support and education are essential

immediate_care: Immediate specialist review for lepra reactions or nerve function impairment
High-dose corticosteroids for Type 1 reaction (reversal reaction) — nerve preservation priority
medications: WHO MDT — Multibacillary (MB): Dapsone 100mg daily + Clofazimine 50mg daily + Rifampicin 600mg + Clofazimine 300mg monthly for 12 months
Oral prednisolone 40–60mg/day for Type 1 lepra reaction — tapered over 3–6 months
Thalidomide 100–400mg/day for Type 2 (ENL) reaction — strict teratogenicity protocols
environmental_precautions: Regular wound care for any plantar ulcers or injuries to anaesthetic sites
Podiatric support for management of foot deformities
lifestyle_adjustments: Occupational therapy for hand function rehabilitation
Community-based rehabilitation for social reintegration
Nutritional support to optimise immune function
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any new neurological deficit — early corticosteroid treatment can prevent permanent paralysis
Eye closure difficulty (lagophthalmos) — urgent ophthalmological care to prevent blindness
warnings: Thalidomide for ENL requires strict contraception monitoring — teratogenicity is severe
Untreated lepra reactions cause irreversible peripheral nerve damage and disability

immediate_care: Hospital admission for severe lepra reactions, systemic ENL, or sepsis
Multidisciplinary team: infectious disease, neurology, ophthalmology, rehabilitation
Emergency corticosteroids for acute nerve function loss
medications: IV methylprednisolone for severe Type 1 reactions with acute nerve palsy
Clofazimine added for ENL prophylaxis in frequently relapsing cases
Surgical decompression for nerve abscesses — specialist neurosurgery
environmental_precautions: Total foot protection and offloading for plantar ulcers
Sterile wound management for trophic ulcers
lifestyle_adjustments: Long-term rehabilitation planning for permanent disabilities (clawing, anaesthesia)
Community support and anti-stigma advocacy
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Acute blindness, facial nerve palsy, or loss of hand grip — emergency specialist care
warnings: Disability from leprosy is preventable with early detection and MDT compliance
Leprosy is NOT a death sentence — early treatment leads to complete cure with minimal disability

Lichen Disorder.description: Group of inflammatory skin conditions including lichen planus and lichen sclerosus

immediate_care: Dermatology review for confirmation of type (lichen planus, lichen sclerosus, lichen simplex)
Apply high-potency topical corticosteroid to affected areas
Identify and eliminate potential triggers (drugs, hepatitis C, stress)
medications: Topical clobetasol 0.05% ointment once daily for 4–8 weeks
Topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment for steroid-sparing maintenance
Antihistamines (hydroxyzine 25mg at night) for pruritus-dominant lichen simplex
environmental_precautions: Avoid irritants: perfumed soaps, synthetic underwear, wet wipes in genital lichen sclerosus
Wear loose, 100% cotton underwear for genital or perianal involvement
Avoid thermal trauma and friction to affected sites
lifestyle_adjustments: Hepatitis C screening for oral lichen planus — strong epidemiological association
Stress reduction as lichen planus is frequently exacerbated by psychological stress
Oral hygiene review if oral mucosa involved
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Genital lichen sclerosus — annual review; long-term risk of squamous cell carcinoma
Oral lichen planus with white striations — biopsy to exclude leukoplakia
If blistering or erosions develop — erosive variant requires urgent management
warnings: Lichen sclerosus carries a 4–6% lifetime risk of SCC at affected sites — regular monitoring mandatory
Genital lichen sclerosus: do not use topical oestrogen as primary treatment — corticosteroids are first-line

immediate_care: Specialist dermatology management for erosive or widespread lichen planus
Oral medicine referral for erosive oral lichen planus
Gynaecology referral for symptomatic genital lichen sclerosus
medications: Topical tacrolimus 0.1% for erosive oral lichen planus (off-label but evidence-based)
Intralesional triamcinolone for hypertrophic lichen planus
Oral prednisolone 30–40mg/day (tapering) for severe erosive cutaneous lichen planus
Acitretin or hydroxychloroquine for refractory lichen planus — specialist
environmental_precautions: Patch testing to identify contact allergens triggering lichenoid reactions
Dental amalgam review for oral lichen planus — amalgam contact reactions documented
lifestyle_adjustments: Soft diet for severe oral lichen planus to reduce mucosal trauma
Psychological support — chronic itch and pain significantly impact quality of life
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Difficulty eating, swallowing, or speaking due to oral involvement
Persistent erosive vulval or penile disease despite topical therapy
warnings: Drug-induced lichenoid reactions: review all medications including antihypertensives, diuretics, and antimalarials

immediate_care: Hospital-based management for ulcerative or widespread erosive lichen disease
Multidisciplinary MDT: dermatology, oral medicine, gynaecology, ophthalmology
Biopsy for histological confirmation and SCC exclusion
medications: Oral cyclosporine for refractory erosive lichen planus
Rituximab or baricitinib (JAK inhibitor) — emerging evidence for severe lichen planus
Topical tacrolimus + topical steroid combination for erosive genital disease
environmental_precautions: Hospital-grade wound dressings for ulcerated erosive disease
Strict genital hygiene without irritants
lifestyle_adjustments: Long-term dermatological surveillance given SCC risk at chronically inflamed sites
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any new induration or nodularity within lichen sclerosus — biopsy immediately (SCC risk)
Rapid deterioration or systemic symptoms
warnings: Chronic active oral lichen planus has malignant potential — minimum 6-monthly review

Lupus Erythematosus.description: Autoimmune condition with cutaneous (DLE/SCLE) and systemic (SLE) manifestations

immediate_care: Comprehensive sun protection is non-negotiable — UV triggers and worsens cutaneous lupus
Dermatology referral for disease classification and monitoring plan
Baseline bloods: ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement C3/C4, FBC, renal and liver function
medications: Hydroxychloroquine 200–400mg daily (first-line for cutaneous and systemic lupus) — 6-8 weeks onset
Topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05%) for active discoid lesions
Topical tacrolimus 0.1% for facial lesions to avoid steroid atrophy
environmental_precautions: Broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen (both UVA and UVB) reapplied every 2 hours
Physical sun barriers: hats, long sleeves, UPF 50+ clothing
Avoid fluorescent and LED lighting exposure if photosensitive — can trigger flares
lifestyle_adjustments: Smoking cessation — tobacco reduces hydroxychloroquine efficacy and worsens lupus
Vitamin D supplementation (1000–2000 IU daily) — deficiency common due to sun avoidance
Stress management — psychological stress is a recognised lupus flare trigger
when_to_seek_medical_attention: New systemic symptoms: fever, joint pain, chest pain, haematuria, headache
Ophthalmology annual review mandatory with hydroxychloroquine (retinal toxicity monitoring)
Baseline and annual renal function, blood pressure, and urinalysis
warnings: Hydroxychloroquine retinopathy is dose and duration dependent — annual ophthalmological review mandatory
Lupus can affect kidneys, brain, heart — systemic features require urgent investigation

immediate_care: Rheumatology + dermatology co-management for systemic lupus (SLE)
Urgent investigation if new renal, neurological, or cardiac features present
Consider hospital review if systemic flare suspected
medications: Oral prednisolone 20–40mg/day for acute systemic flare — taper over 8–12 weeks
Hydroxychloroquine continued alongside corticosteroids
Azathioprine 2mg/kg/day or mycophenolate mofetil 1.5–3g/day as steroid-sparing agents
Topical treatments continued for skin involvement
environmental_precautions: Absolute sun avoidance during active flares
Infection prevention — immunosuppression increases infection risk
lifestyle_adjustments: Rheumatology follow-up every 3 months during active disease
Bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate) with prolonged corticosteroid use
Pregnancy planning under specialist supervision — lupus is high-risk in pregnancy
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any new neuropsychiatric symptoms — lupus cerebritis is an emergency
Declining renal function or new proteinuria — urgent nephrology review
warnings: Antiphospholipid syndrome co-occurs in 20-40% of SLE — clotting risk must be assessed
Pregnancy in SLE requires specialist obstetric and rheumatology co-management

immediate_care: Emergency hospital admission for lupus nephritis, cerebritis, or severe cytopenias
IV methylprednisolone 500–1000mg for 3 days (pulse therapy) for organ-threatening disease
MDT: rheumatology, nephrology, neurology, haematology
medications: IV cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus protocol) for lupus nephritis
Mycophenolate mofetil for lupus nephritis maintenance
Belimumab (anti-BLyS biologic) for refractory SLE — specialist only
Anifrolumab (anti-interferon biologic) for moderate-severe refractory cutaneous/systemic lupus
environmental_precautions: Strict infection prevention — cyclophosphamide causes significant immunosuppression
PCP prophylaxis with co-trimoxazole during intensive immunosuppression
lifestyle_adjustments: Dialysis preparation if lupus nephritis progresses to ESRD
Multidisciplinary psychological support — severe lupus profoundly affects quality of life
when_to_seek_medical_attention: All severe lupus organ involvement requires immediate specialist inpatient care
warnings: Cyclophosphamide gonadotoxicity — fertility preservation discussion before treatment
Lupus cerebritis and severe nephritis carry significant morbidity and mortality

Moles.description: Common benign melanocytic naevi — monitor for atypical features (ABCDE)

immediate_care: Apply the ABCDE rule: Asymmetry, Border, Colour, Diameter >6mm, Evolution
Photograph moles with a ruler for baseline documentation
Dermatoscopy by dermatologist to assess benign vs atypical features
medications: No pharmacological treatment required for typical benign moles
Daily SPF 50+ sunscreen to all moles, especially sun-exposed sites
environmental_precautions: Strict UV protection — sun exposure can induce malignant transformation of melanocytes
Avoid tanning beds — increase melanoma risk significantly
lifestyle_adjustments: Monthly self-examination using ABCDE checklist
Annual full-body skin check with dermatologist if >50 moles or atypical naevi
Avoid irritating moles with tight clothing or jewellery
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any mole that changes (ABCDE criteria) — urgent dermatology within 2 weeks
