Mole that bleeds spontaneously, itches persistently, or develops satellite lesions
Family history of melanoma — enhanced surveillance programme required
warnings: Dysplastic (atypical) naevi are not cancerous but indicate increased melanoma risk
Never attempt home removal of moles — risk of incomplete excision and missed melanoma

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology referral for excision biopsy of atypical or changing moles
Dermoscopic assessment to guide excision priority
Total body photography for high-risk patients with multiple dysplastic naevi
medications: Post-excision wound care: topical antibiotic and non-adherent dressing
Silicone gel for scar management post-excision
environmental_precautions: Maximum UV protection post-excision to prevent hyperpigmented scarring
Protect surgical site from trauma during healing
lifestyle_adjustments: Lifestyle sun-safety education for all family members
3–6 monthly dermatology review for patients with multiple atypical naevi
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Histopathology showing moderate or severe dysplasia — wider re-excision may be required
Post-excision site changes: non-healing wound or regrowth of pigmentation
warnings: Histopathological analysis of ALL excised moles is mandatory

immediate_care: Urgent referral to skin cancer MDT if melanoma suspected
Sentinel lymph node biopsy for melanoma staging if Breslow thickness >0.8mm
Full-body CT/PET imaging for suspected metastatic disease
medications: BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib + trametinib) for BRAF V600E mutation-positive melanoma
Immunotherapy: nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or ipilimumab + nivolumab for metastatic melanoma
Adjuvant immunotherapy for high-risk resected melanoma
environmental_precautions: Absolute sun protection; sunscreen alone insufficient for high-risk patients
lifestyle_adjustments: Melanoma MDT lifelong follow-up
Genetic testing for familial melanoma syndromes (CDKN2A mutation)
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any new pigmented lesion, lymph node swelling, or systemic symptoms post-melanoma diagnosis
warnings: Melanoma is the most lethal skin cancer — early detection is life-saving
All first-degree relatives of melanoma patients require annual skin screening

Molluscum Contagiosum.description: Common viral skin infection (poxvirus) causing pearly papules, predominantly in children

immediate_care: Reassure — most cases in immunocompetent children resolve spontaneously within 6–18 months
Avoid squeezing or scratching lesions — accelerates spread and causes secondary infection
Keep lesions covered with clothing where possible to reduce transmission
medications: Watchful waiting is acceptable first-line management for most children
Topical potassium hydroxide 10% solution applied to lesions (treatment-seeking cases)
Topical imiquimod 5% cream three times weekly for immunocompromised patients
environmental_precautions: Do not share towels, swimwear, or skin contact items
Cover lesions during swimming — use waterproof plasters
Avoid contact sports during active infection to prevent transmission
lifestyle_adjustments: Keep nails short to prevent auto-inoculation
Educate parents — condition is benign and self-limiting in immunocompetent children
Discourage shaving over affected areas — spreads lesions
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If lesions become inflamed, painful, or show signs of secondary bacterial infection
If lesions are located on eyelids — ophthalmology referral (risk of conjunctivitis)
Adults with widespread molluscum — consider HIV testing (may indicate immunosuppression)
warnings: Widespread molluscum contagiosum in adults may indicate HIV or other immunosuppression — investigate
Eczema patients are at risk of widespread molluscum — treat underlying eczema to restore skin barrier

immediate_care: Dermatology review for widespread, recalcitrant, or facial molluscum
Cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen) for discrete lesions in cooperative patients
Curettage under topical anaesthesia (EMLA cream) for rapid clearance
medications: Topical cantharidin 0.7% applied in clinic (blistering agent) — effective for multiple lesions
Topical tretinoin 0.025–0.05% cream applied nightly — promotes immune response
Oral cimetidine 40mg/kg/day for 2–3 months (immunomodulatory — limited evidence)
environmental_precautions: Avoid communal changing rooms, gyms, and swimming pools during treatment
Enhanced household hygiene to prevent spread to siblings
lifestyle_adjustments: Psychological support for school-aged children experiencing social difficulties
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Treatment failure with topical therapy — cryotherapy or curettage needed
Conjunctival involvement — urgent ophthalmology review

immediate_care: Urgent HIV testing and immunological workup for widespread molluscum in adults
Dermatology specialist management for immunocompromised patients
Cidofovir or imiquimod for refractory molluscum in HIV-positive patients
medications: Topical cidofovir 1–3% for immunocompromised refractory cases
HAART optimisation in HIV-positive patients — immune reconstitution often clears molluscum
Combination cryotherapy + topical imiquimod for resistant cases
environmental_precautions: Infection control guidance for immunocompromised inpatients with molluscum
lifestyle_adjustments: HIV clinic co-management for optimising immune status
Ongoing dermatology review every 3 months for immunocompromised patients
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Rapid spread or giant molluscum (>5mm) in immunocompromised patient — urgent specialist care
warnings: Widespread molluscum in HIV: CD4 count <200 cells/mm³ — ART intensification is primary treatment

Nevus.description: Benign melanocytic naevus (mole) — requires monitoring for atypical changes

immediate_care: Apply ABCDE criteria assessment and photograph for baseline
Reassure patient if features are entirely typical (symmetrical, well-defined border, uniform colour)
Annual dermatoscopic review recommended
medications: No pharmacological treatment required
Daily broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen application
environmental_precautions: Strict UV avoidance and sun protection measures
Avoid tanning beds
lifestyle_adjustments: Monthly self-examination using the ABCDE checklist
Annual dermatology skin check
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any ABCDE change, spontaneous bleeding, or new symptoms
Strong family history of melanoma — enhanced surveillance required
warnings: Do not attempt home removal — risk of incomplete excision and delayed melanoma diagnosis

immediate_care: Dermatology review for atypical or changing naevus — excision biopsy may be required
medications: Post-excision wound care as directed by clinician
environmental_precautions: Maximum UV protection at excision site during healing
lifestyle_adjustments: 3–6 monthly dermatology review for multiple or atypical naevi
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Histopathology showing dysplasia — re-excision may be indicated
warnings: All excised naevi require histopathological analysis

immediate_care: Urgent skin cancer MDT referral if melanoma suspected on biopsy
medications: Oncological therapy as per melanoma protocols if malignancy confirmed
environmental_precautions: Absolute sun protection and lifelong melanoma surveillance
lifestyle_adjustments: MDT follow-up; genetic counselling for familial melanoma
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Immediate oncology referral if melanoma confirmed
warnings: Giant congenital naevi carry lifetime melanoma risk of up to 10% — require specialist surveillance

Normal.description: No skin disease detected — healthy skin

immediate_care: No treatment required — maintain good skin hygiene
Cleanse face twice daily with a gentle pH-balanced cleanser
Apply appropriate moisturiser for your skin type after cleansing
medications: No medications required for healthy skin
Consider daily SPF 30+ as preventive photoprotection
environmental_precautions: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30+ daily — UV damage is cumulative and causes premature ageing
Wear protective clothing and seek shade during peak UV hours (10am–4pm)
Stay hydrated to maintain skin moisture from within
lifestyle_adjustments: Maintain balanced diet rich in antioxidants (fruits, vegetables, omega-3 fatty acids)
Adequate sleep (7–9 hours) supports skin barrier regeneration
Manage stress — cortisol elevation impairs skin barrier and immunity
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Annual skin check if >50 moles, family history of skin cancer, or significant sun exposure history
If any new, changing, or unusual skin lesion develops

immediate_care: Maintain regular skincare and sun protection routine
medications: SPF 50+ sunscreen daily as photoprotective measure
environmental_precautions: Consistent UV protection all year round
lifestyle_adjustments: Healthy balanced lifestyle for optimal skin health
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Annual dermatology skin check recommended

immediate_care: No skin disease detected — no acute intervention required
medications: No medications indicated
environmental_precautions: Maintain photoprotection as standard practice
lifestyle_adjustments: Continue healthy lifestyle and skincare routine
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Seek medical advice if skin symptoms develop

Pityriasis Rosea.description: Self-limiting viral exanthem causing herald patch followed by secondary rash

immediate_care: Reassure — pityriasis rosea is self-limiting, resolving in 6–12 weeks
Identify and photograph the herald patch (initial larger oval plaque)
Avoid hot baths and heavy exercise — heat worsens pruritus and rash
medications: Oral antihistamine (cetirizine 10mg) for symptomatic itch relief
Topical menthol lotion or calamine lotion for cooling pruritic relief
Moderate-potency topical corticosteroid (betamethasone valerate 0.1%) for severe itch
environmental_precautions: Wear loose, breathable clothing to minimise friction and sweating
Lukewarm (not hot) showers with gentle soap-free wash
Avoid strong sunlight — though controlled UV exposure may hasten resolution
lifestyle_adjustments: Rest adequately — some patients experience viral prodromal fatigue
Emollient application to maintain skin moisture and reduce scale
Pityriasis rosea is not contagious to others — no isolation required
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If rash persists beyond 12 weeks — exclude pityriasis lichenoides or drug eruption
Secondary syphilis can mimic pityriasis rosea — syphilis serology if any doubt
Pregnancy: pityriasis rosea has been linked to adverse outcomes — obstetric review recommended
warnings: Pityriasis rosea in pregnancy has been associated with premature delivery — advise obstetric review
Drug-induced pityriasis rosea-like eruption: review all medications (ACE inhibitors, gold, bismuth)

immediate_care: Dermatology review if extensive, very pruritic, or atypical distribution
Narrow-band UVB phototherapy (3 sessions per week) dramatically reduces duration and itch
Syphilis serology to exclude secondary syphilis if clinical uncertainty
medications: Oral aciclovir 800mg five times daily for 1 week — may shorten duration (HHV-7 association)
Oral erythromycin 250mg four times daily for 2 weeks (limited evidence; occasionally used)
Oral prednisolone short course for very severe pruritus — specialist directed
environmental_precautions: Controlled sun exposure (10–15 min daily of natural sunlight) may accelerate resolution
Avoid heavy exercise and saunas during active eruption
lifestyle_adjustments: Diet rich in anti-inflammatory foods during recovery phase
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Worsening despite 4 weeks of treatment
Pregnancy with pityriasis rosea — foetal monitoring via obstetrics

immediate_care: Dermatology review for severe, vesicular, or systemic pityriasis rosea
Exclude drug eruption and syphilis with appropriate investigations
medications: Narrow-band UVB phototherapy series for severe pruritic cases
Systemic corticosteroids for extremely widespread or vesicular variants — short course
environmental_precautions: Protect skin from secondary infection at excoriated sites
lifestyle_adjustments: Recovery typically complete within 12 weeks — patience and symptomatic management key
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If systemic symptoms (fever, arthralgia) persist — exclude other differential diagnoses
warnings: Inverse pityriasis rosea (axillary/groin predominance) is easily confused with other diagnoses — dermatology review

Rosacea.description: Chronic inflammatory facial skin condition causing redness, papules, and telangiectasia

immediate_care: Identify and eliminate personal rosacea triggers (spicy food, alcohol, hot drinks, sunlight, stress)
Use gentle, soap-free cleanser and fragrance-free moisturiser twice daily
Apply SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen (titanium dioxide or zinc oxide) daily — UV is a universal trigger
medications: Topical metronidazole 0.75% gel or cream twice daily (first-line for papulopustular rosacea)
Topical azelaic acid 15% gel twice daily — equally effective, useful in pregnancy
Topical ivermectin 1% cream once daily (superior efficacy for papulopustular subtype)
environmental_precautions: Avoid extreme temperatures, saunas, steam rooms, and hot baths
Use mineral (not chemical) sunscreen — chemical filters may irritate rosacea-prone skin
Wear wide-brim hat outdoors; avoid wind exposure with barrier scarves
lifestyle_adjustments: Keep a symptom diary to identify personal triggers
Avoid vasodilatory foods: spicy food, alcohol, caffeine, hot beverages
Use only rosacea-safe, fragrance-free, non-comedogenic cosmetics
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If papules and pustules do not respond to 8 weeks of topical therapy
If eye symptoms develop (ocular rosacea: eye redness, grittiness) — ophthalmology review
Rhinophyma (nasal enlargement) — specialist surgical treatment available
warnings: Topical corticosteroids WORSEN rosacea — avoid completely
Ocular rosacea can cause corneal damage if untreated — report any eye symptoms urgently

immediate_care: Dermatology review for papulopustular or phymatous rosacea
Oral antibiotic therapy alongside topical maintenance
Vascular laser for persistent erythema and telangiectasia — specialist referral
medications: Oral doxycycline 40mg (modified-release, sub-antimicrobial dose) once daily for 12–16 weeks
Oral doxycycline 100mg daily for severe papulopustular rosacea (standard dose, 8–12 weeks)
Topical ivermectin 1% for maintenance after oral antibiotic induction
Brimonidine tartrate 0.33% gel for persistent facial erythema (flushing subtype)
environmental_precautions: Strict trigger avoidance and sun protection
Cool compresses during flushing episodes for immediate relief
lifestyle_adjustments: Alcohol avoidance — ethanol is a potent rosacea trigger
Green-tinted primer under makeup can neutralise facial redness cosmetically
when_to_seek_medical_attention: No improvement after 16 weeks of doxycycline — consider isotretinoin
Progressive rhinophyma — CO2 laser or surgical reshaping available
warnings: Doxycycline: photosensitivity — rigorous sun protection mandatory during treatment
Avoid topical steroids entirely in rosacea — causes steroid rosacea (perioral dermatitis)

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology review for severe phymatous or recalcitrant papulopustular rosacea
Isotretinoin candidacy assessment
Ophthalmology emergency referral if ocular rosacea threatens corneal integrity
medications: Oral isotretinoin 0.3–0.5mg/kg/day for severe papulopustular rosacea refractory to antibiotics
Oral ivermectin (off-label) — emerging use in Demodex-driven severe rosacea
CO2 or Er:YAG laser for established rhinophyma — specialist surgical clinic
environmental_precautions: Isotretinoin: absolute photoprotection, SPF 50+ mandatory
Avoid all identified triggers rigorously during isotretinoin course
lifestyle_adjustments: Isotretinoin monitoring: monthly LFTs, lipids, FBC, and pregnancy tests in females
Psychological support — severe rosacea significantly impacts facial appearance and self-esteem
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Corneal ulceration from ocular rosacea — ophthalmological emergency
Isotretinoin side effects: severe mucocutaneous toxicity, vision changes, mood changes
warnings: Isotretinoin teratogenicity — mandatory contraception programme (Pregnancy Prevention Programme) for all females of childbearing age

Squamous cell carcinoma.description: Second most common skin cancer; may metastasise if left untreated

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology referral within 1–2 weeks — SCC requires histological confirmation
Biopsy or shave excision for tissue diagnosis
Document lesion size, location, and lymph node status
medications: Topical imiquimod or fluorouracil for Bowen's disease (SCC in situ) only
Cryotherapy for small SCC in situ under dermatologist supervision
No topical treatment for invasive SCC — surgical excision is mandatory
environmental_precautions: Strict UV protection: SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen, UPF clothing, hat, shade
Avoid all tanning bed exposure — UV is the primary carcinogenic driver
Avoid chemical carcinogens: arsenic, tar, industrial chemicals
lifestyle_adjustments: Annual full-body skin checks
HPV vaccination discussed with specialist for HPV-driven SCC (anogenital sites)
Smoking cessation for lip and oral SCC
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any rapidly growing, indurated, or ulcerated lesion — urgent biopsy
Lymph node enlargement near SCC site — potential metastasis
Immunosuppressed patients: 6-monthly skin checks (100x higher SCC risk)
warnings: SCC in organ transplant recipients is aggressive with high metastatic potential
Perineural or lymphovascular invasion requires radiotherapy adjuvant — specialist MDT

immediate_care: Surgical excision with 4–6mm margin within 2–4 weeks of diagnosis
Sentinel lymph node biopsy if high-risk features present
CT scan for regional lymph node and metastasis assessment
medications: Adjuvant radiotherapy post-excision for positive margins or perineural invasion
Post-operative wound care: topical antibiotic and moist wound dressing
Cemiplimab (anti-PD-1) for locally advanced unresectable SCC
environmental_precautions: Continued strict photoprotection post-excision
Oral nicotinamide 500mg twice daily for chemoprevention of new SCCs in high-risk patients
lifestyle_adjustments: 3-monthly dermatology review for 2 years post-excision; then 6-monthly
Skin cancer support group referral
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any local recurrence at excision site — urgent re-referral
New lymphadenopathy or systemic symptoms — staging CT scan required
warnings: Mohs surgery recommended for high-risk anatomical sites (face, ears, hands, scalp)

immediate_care: Emergency skin cancer MDT for locally advanced, nodal, or metastatic SCC
PET-CT scan for staging of suspected metastatic SCC
Multidisciplinary planning: dermatological surgery, oncology, radiotherapy
medications: Cemiplimab or pembrolizumab (PD-1 inhibitors) for advanced/metastatic SCC
Platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin + 5-FU) for PD-1 inhibitor-ineligible patients
EGFR inhibitors (cetuximab) as alternative systemic option
environmental_precautions: Immunotherapy requires infection screening and side-effect monitoring
Absolute UV protection during and after treatment
lifestyle_adjustments: Palliative care team involvement for symptom management in advanced disease
Psychological oncology support
when_to_seek_medical_attention: All advanced SCC requires immediate specialist oncology management
warnings: Metastatic SCC prognosis is poor — immunotherapy has significantly improved outcomes but specialist management is essential

Sun Sunlight Damage.description: Cumulative UV-induced skin changes including photoageing, solar lentigines, and field cancerisation

immediate_care: Implement rigorous daily photoprotection — the single most important intervention
Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen every morning (and reapply every 2 hours outdoors)
Dermatology review for assessment of any actinic keratoses within damaged skin
medications: Topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.025–0.05%) nightly — proven to reverse photoageing
Topical vitamin C serum (15–20%) in the morning — antioxidant protection and collagen stimulation
Topical azelaic acid or kojic acid for solar lentigine (age spot) lightening
environmental_precautions: Daily SPF 50+ sunscreen, even in cloudy conditions and indoors near windows
UPF 50+ protective clothing, wide-brim hat, and UV-blocking sunglasses
Peak UV hours avoidance (10am–4pm); seek shade proactively
lifestyle_adjustments: Antioxidant-rich diet: vitamins C and E, carotenoids, polyphenols — complement topical therapy
Stay well-hydrated — skin hydration supports barrier and repair functions
Smoking cessation — tobacco compounds UV photodamage significantly
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Annual dermatology review for actinic keratosis surveillance and treatment
Any new rough, scaly, or thickened area within sun-damaged skin — biopsy if uncertain
Sudden appearance of multiple new lentigines or seborrhoeic keratoses (sign of Leser-Trélat)
warnings: Cumulative UV damage cannot be fully reversed — prevention of further damage is the priority
Field cancerisation increases risk of multiple SCCs over time — regular surveillance essential

immediate_care: Field therapy for actinic keratosis-predominant sun damage: topical fluorouracil or imiquimod
Photodynamic therapy (PDT) for widespread actinic field cancerisation
Chemical peel or laser resurfacing for significant photoageing — specialist dermatology
medications: Topical fluorouracil 5% cream — apply twice daily for 2–4 weeks for field treatment of AK
Topical imiquimod 5% cream — 3 times weekly for actinic field cancerisation
Oral nicotinamide 500mg twice daily — reduces new AK formation in high-risk patients
environmental_precautions: UV-blocking window film for vehicles and offices with high sun exposure
Consider UV-protective clothing for daily wear if occupation involves outdoor exposure
lifestyle_adjustments: 3–6 monthly dermatology review during field therapy and 6-monthly thereafter
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Treatment failure or emergence of indurated, ulcerated lesions within field — SCC biopsy
Patient with history of immunosuppression or transplant — 3-monthly reviews
warnings: Fluorouracil and imiquimod cause intense inflammation during treatment — expected and therapeutic
Photosensitivity is markedly increased during field therapy — absolute sun avoidance required

immediate_care: MDT skin cancer review for established field cancerisation with multiple SCCs or in situ lesions
Systematic mapping of all lesions with dermoscopy and total body photography
Aggressive field treatment with PDT series, followed by oral chemoprevention
medications: Oral acitretin (systemic retinoid) for chemoprevention in very high-risk patients
Systemic biologics or checkpoint inhibitors for confirmed invasive SCC within damaged field
Pain management for treatment-related reactions
environmental_precautions: Consider geographic relocation away from high UV index environments for very high-risk patients
Complete UV abstinence; sun-protective clothing as baseline standard
lifestyle_adjustments: Lifelong dermatological surveillance: minimum 6-monthly reviews
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any new lesion within sun-damaged skin with atypical features — urgent biopsy
warnings: Organ transplant recipients with severe photodamage require chemoprevention and intensive surveillance — SCC mortality risk is significant in this group

Tinea.description: Dermatophyte fungal infections including tinea pedis (athlete's foot), tinea corporis (ringworm), tinea capitis, and tinea unguium (onychomycosis)

immediate_care: Confirm clinical diagnosis — tinea corporis has a classic annular, scaly, pruritic plaque
Keep affected areas clean and thoroughly dry — moisture promotes dermatophyte growth
Apply topical antifungal cream to affected area and 2cm beyond the border
medications: Topical terbinafine 1% cream once daily for 1–2 weeks (tinea corporis/pedis — first-line)
Topical clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 2–4 weeks as alternative
Topical miconazole 2% powder for tinea pedis — reduces moisture in web spaces
environmental_precautions: Wear breathable cotton socks; change after sweating (tinea pedis)
Do not share towels, shoes, or clothing — highly transmissible by fomites
Disinfect shower floors; wear flip-flops in communal changing areas
lifestyle_adjustments: Dry feet thoroughly after bathing — especially between toes
Use antifungal powder in shoes during active infection
Wash all socks at >60°C and use separate towel for affected area
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If infection does not resolve after 4 weeks of topical therapy
Tinea capitis (scalp) always requires oral systemic antifungal treatment
Tinea unguium (nail) — topical therapy is insufficient; oral therapy required
warnings: Tinea capitis in children requires oral griseofulvin or terbinafine — topicals do not penetrate hair follicle
Topical corticosteroid-antifungal combinations (e.g. Lotriderm) can mask tinea and worsen it — use with caution

immediate_care: Dermatology or GP review for widespread, treatment-resistant, or nail tinea
Skin scraping (KOH preparation) and fungal culture for confirmation
Initiate oral antifungal for extensive or follicular tinea
medications: Oral terbinafine 250mg daily for 2–6 weeks (tinea corporis, pedis) — first-line
Oral itraconazole 100–200mg daily for 2–4 weeks as alternative
Oral griseofulvin 10–20mg/kg/day for tinea capitis (especially in children — Microsporum)
Topical amorolfine nail lacquer 5% weekly for mild onychomycosis (fingernails)
environmental_precautions: Environmental decontamination: vacuum carpets, disinfect shoes with antifungal spray
Treat all affected household members and pets simultaneously for tinea capitis
lifestyle_adjustments: Avoid communal swimming pools, gyms, and changing areas until infection cleared
Footwear hygiene: replace heavily contaminated shoes during treatment
when_to_seek_medical_attention: No response to oral terbinafine after 4 weeks — consider terbinafine-resistant dermatophytes
Kerion (inflammatory tinea capitis) — urgent treatment to prevent scarring alopecia
warnings: Terbinafine: hepatotoxicity risk — LFTs before and during prolonged therapy
Kerion (boggy inflammatory mass on scalp) requires urgent oral antifungal + oral steroids to prevent permanent hair loss

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology review for widespread tinea or treatment-resistant onychomycosis
Fungal culture and sensitivity for suspected resistant dermatophytes (T. violaceum, T. tonsurans)
Immunodeficiency workup if extensive or unusual tinea presentation in adult
medications: Oral terbinafine 250mg daily for 6 weeks (fingernails) or 12 weeks (toenails) for onychomycosis
Itraconazole pulse therapy: 200mg twice daily for 1 week per month x 3 months
Voriconazole for rare azole-resistant or non-dermatophyte mould onychomycosis — specialist only
environmental_precautions: Thorough environmental decontamination and continued footwear hygiene
Nail avulsion (partial or complete) may be required alongside systemic therapy for severe onychomycosis
lifestyle_adjustments: Podiatry referral for nail management in severe onychomycosis
Diabetic patients: podiatry co-management essential — tinea pedis and onychomycosis carry cellulitis risk
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Secondary bacterial cellulitis complicating tinea pedis — urgent antibiotics required
Diabetic patient with tinea pedis — higher-risk pathway, dermatology + podiatry co-management
warnings: Untreated tinea pedis in diabetic patients can progress to cellulitis and limb-threatening infection
Systemic antifungals have significant drug interactions — review all medications before initiating

Vascular Tumors or Lesion.description: Benign or malignant vascular lesions including haemangiomas, pyogenic granulomas, and Kaposi's sarcoma

immediate_care: Dermatology review for accurate classification of vascular lesion type
Dermoscopy to distinguish benign haemangioma from pyogenic granuloma or KS
Photograph lesion with ruler for baseline documentation
medications: Topical timolol 0.5% gel for superficial infantile haemangiomas — apply twice daily
No pharmacological treatment for typical cherry angiomas
Topical imiquimod 5% for selected pyogenic granulomas — off-label
environmental_precautions: Protect vascular lesions from trauma — they bleed easily
Avoid compression or friction over lesions
lifestyle_adjustments: Parents of children with infantile haemangiomas should monitor for airway, eye, or liver involvement
Annual dermatology review for all vascular lesions not requiring immediate treatment
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Rapidly growing pyogenic granuloma — bleeds profusely and requires treatment
Infantile haemangioma involving airway, eye, or >5 lesions — specialist urgent review
Any suspicion of Kaposi's sarcoma (purple vascular plaques) — HIV testing and oncology referral
warnings: Kaposi's sarcoma is HIV-associated — any suspicious lesion requires HIV testing
Pyogenic granulomas bleed severely with minimal trauma — keep protected and treat promptly

immediate_care: Oral propranolol for problematic infantile haemangiomas — paediatric dermatology supervised
Curettage and cautery for pyogenic granuloma — fast and effective
Laser therapy (pulsed dye laser) for port-wine stains and telangiectasia
medications: Oral propranolol 1–3mg/kg/day for infantile haemangioma (first-line systemic treatment)
Oral prednisolone (alternative to propranolol) for haemangiomas with propranolol contraindications
Sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor) for vascular anomalies (lymphatic malformations) — specialist only
environmental_precautions: Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs — increase bleeding risk from vascular lesions
Post-laser therapy: sun protection essential to prevent dyspigmentation
lifestyle_adjustments: Psychological support for patients with facial vascular birthmarks
Referral to vascular anomaly MDT centre for complex lesions
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Haemorrhage from vascular lesion — compress and seek emergency care
Rapid growth of any vascular lesion — malignancy exclusion needed
warnings: Propranolol for infantile haemangioma: risk of hypoglycaemia and hypotension — monitoring mandatory
Haemangiomas in beard distribution may indicate subglottic haemangioma — airway imaging required

immediate_care: Emergency referral for Kaposi's sarcoma, angiosarcoma, or uncontrolled haemorrhage
Full HIV workup for KS; staging CT for angiosarcoma
MDT: dermatology, oncology, vascular surgery
medications: Liposomal doxorubicin for disseminated Kaposi's sarcoma
HAART optimisation for HIV-KS (immune reconstitution often results in KS regression)
Paclitaxel or bevacizumab for cutaneous angiosarcoma — oncologist supervised
environmental_precautions: Strict wound care and infection prevention for ulcerated vascular tumours
Avoid trauma to active tumour sites
lifestyle_adjustments: Palliative care team involvement for advanced vascular malignancies
Psychological support and end-of-life planning if appropriate
when_to_seek_medical_attention: All malignant vascular tumours require immediate specialist oncology management
warnings: Cutaneous angiosarcoma carries extremely poor prognosis — early aggressive treatment is essential

Vasculitis.description: Inflammation of blood vessel walls causing palpable purpura, ulcers, and systemic involvement

immediate_care: Urgent dermatology and rheumatology assessment — vasculitis may indicate systemic disease
Blood tests: ANCA, ANA, complement, cryoglobulins, hepatitis B/C, FBC, renal and liver function
Urine dipstick for haematuria/proteinuria — renal vasculitis exclusion
medications: Identify and remove triggering cause (drug withdrawal, infection treatment)
Leg elevation and compression for dependent palpable purpura
Colchicine 0.5–1mg twice daily or dapsone 50mg daily for urticarial vasculitis
environmental_precautions: Elevate legs above heart level when resting to reduce dependent purpura
Avoid prolonged standing and cold exposure — both worsen cutaneous vasculitis
Compression stockings for lower limb vasculitis
lifestyle_adjustments: Identify and eliminate triggering infections or medications
Alcohol avoidance if cryoglobulinaemia-associated vasculitis
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Any renal, pulmonary, neurological, or gastrointestinal involvement — emergency
Rapidly progressive or necrotic skin lesions — ulcerating vasculitis
ANCA-positive vasculitis — immediate rheumatology assessment for GPA/MPA
warnings: Cutaneous vasculitis may be the presenting feature of systemic vasculitis (GPA, MPA, eosinophilic GPA) — never dismiss as purely cutaneous
Pauci-immune vasculitis with ANCA positivity can cause rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis — emergency investigation required

immediate_care: Hospital assessment for systemic vasculitis or extensive cutaneous involvement
Biopsy of active lesion with direct immunofluorescence
Multi-system assessment: renal, pulmonary, neurological
medications: Oral prednisolone 40–60mg/day for moderate cutaneous vasculitis
Colchicine or hydroxychloroquine for urticarial vasculitis
Immunosuppressants (azathioprine, methotrexate) for steroid-dependent cases — specialist
environmental_precautions: Rigorous wound care for ulcerating vasculitic lesions
Compression bandaging for lower limb ulcers under specialist guidance
lifestyle_adjustments: Regular rheumatology and dermatology co-management
Bone protection with calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate during steroid therapy
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Worsening renal function or new haematuria during treatment — urgent nephrology
Peripheral neuropathy or systemic features developing
warnings: Do not reduce corticosteroids too rapidly — risks disease flare and organ damage
Screen for infections before and during immunosuppression (TB, hepatitis, HIV)

immediate_care: Emergency hospital admission for organ-threatening vasculitis
IV methylprednisolone 500–1000mg pulse therapy for severe ANCA-associated vasculitis
Plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) for severe renal or pulmonary-renal syndrome
medications: IV cyclophosphamide or rituximab for ANCA-associated vasculitis induction
Avacopan (complement C5a receptor inhibitor) as steroid-sparing agent for GPA/MPA
IV immunoglobulin for refractory cases or those with sepsis risk
Dialysis if acute renal failure from vasculitis
environmental_precautions: Strict infection prevention during intensive immunosuppression
PCP prophylaxis with co-trimoxazole during cyclophosphamide therapy
lifestyle_adjustments: MDT approach: rheumatology, nephrology, pulmonology, dermatology
Long-term immunosuppression monitoring and infection vigilance
when_to_seek_medical_attention: All organ-threatening vasculitis is a medical emergency requiring immediate specialist care
warnings: ANCA-associated vasculitis with diffuse alveolar haemorrhage has 50% mortality without aggressive treatment
Rituximab for ANCA vasculitis: risk of hepatitis B reactivation — HBV status mandatory before initiation

Warts.description: Common HPV-induced benign epidermal growths; multiple types including common, plantar, flat, and filiform warts

immediate_care: Reassure — most warts in immunocompetent individuals resolve spontaneously within 2 years
Protect from auto-inoculation: do not pick, bite, or scratch warts
Cover plantar warts (verrucae) during swimming and communal showers
medications: Topical salicylic acid 12–26% applied daily after filing with emery board (first-line self-treatment)
Topical salicylic acid 40% pads for plantar warts — more concentrated for thick callus
Over-counter dimethyl ether (Wartner/Bazuka) cryotherapy — less effective than clinical liquid nitrogen
environmental_precautions: Wear flip-flops in communal changing rooms and pool areas
Do not share towels or footwear with others
Cover broken skin or warts before swimming to reduce HPV transmission
lifestyle_adjustments: Wash hands thoroughly after touching warts
HPV vaccination (Gardasil-9): discuss with GP for genital wart prevention in eligible age groups
Keep warts covered with waterproof plaster to reduce transmission
when_to_seek_medical_attention: If warts fail to respond to 3 months of topical salicylic acid
Genital warts always require medical assessment — specialist STI clinic
If immunocompromised — warts may be extensive and require specialist management
warnings: Genital warts (HPV 6 and 11) are sexually transmitted — partner notification and STI screening required
HPV vaccination before sexual debut provides optimal protection against oncogenic and wart-causing HPV strains

immediate_care: GP or dermatology review for multiple, painful, or treatment-resistant warts
Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen (LN2) every 2–3 weeks until clearance
Mosaic verrucae on soles: consider combination salicylic acid + cryotherapy
medications: Cantharidin 0.7% (blistering agent) applied in clinic — effective for recalcitrant warts
Intralesional bleomycin injections for multiple resistant warts — specialist clinic
Topical imiquimod 5% cream for flat or recalcitrant warts (off-label)
environmental_precautions: Post-cryotherapy: protect blistered area; avoid swimming until healed
Avoid mechanical trauma to post-treatment sites
lifestyle_adjustments: Patience required — average 4–6 cryotherapy sessions for clearance
Psychological support for school-aged children affected by bullying around warts
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Failure after 6 cycles of cryotherapy — second-line treatments available
Periungual warts (around nails) — specialist laser or surgical management
warnings: Cryotherapy is painful — topical anaesthetic (EMLA) recommended for children and plantar verrucae
Periungual warts require careful cryotherapy — risk of nail matrix damage

immediate_care: Dermatology specialist review for extensive, recalcitrant, or immunocompromised-associated warts
Immunological workup for widespread or unusually persistent warts in adults
medications: Intralesional candida antigen immunotherapy — stimulates immune-mediated wart clearance
Oral zinc sulfate 10mg/kg/day (max 600mg/day) for multiple warts — modest evidence
CO2 or pulsed dye laser for recalcitrant warts — specialist laser clinic
Oral cimetidine 25–40mg/kg/day (immunomodulatory) for multiple warts in children
environmental_precautions: Infection control for immunocompromised patients with extensive HPV disease
HPV vaccination of unvaccinated immunocompromised patients — live vaccines avoided
lifestyle_adjustments: Optimisation of immunosuppression where clinically possible
HIV clinic co-management if HIV-associated extensive HPV disease
when_to_seek_medical_attention: Epidermodysplasia verruciformis — specialist management for this rare HPV-susceptibility syndrome
Bowenoid papulosis (HPV-related in situ SCC) — urgent specialist review
warnings: Bowenoid papulosis in genital area may appear wart-like but is high-grade HPV-related dysplasia — biopsy essential
Epidermodysplasia verruciformis carries lifelong risk of cutaneous SCC — annual skin checks
